1-1.text:
Alice was beginning to get very tired of sitting by her sister on the
bank, and of having nothing to do: once or twice she had peeped into
the book her sister was reading, but it had no pictures or
conversations in it, вҖңand what is the use of a book,вҖқ thought Alice
вҖңwithout pictures or conversations?вҖқ

So she was considering in her own mind (as well as she could, for the
hot day made her feel very sleepy and stupid), whether the pleasure of
making a daisy-chain would be worth the trouble of getting up and
picking the daisies, when suddenly a White Rabbit with pink eyes ran
close by her.

There was nothing so very remarkable in that; nor did Alice think it
so very much out of the way to hear the Rabbit say to itself, вҖңOh
dear! Oh dear! I shall be late!вҖқ
1-1.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Chapter I title page ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice bored by the river
1-1.nextIllustration: Alice B. Woodward ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Suddenly a white rabbit ran by

1-2.text:
(when she thought it over afterwards,
it occurred to her that she ought to have wondered at this, but at the
time it all seemed quite natural); but when the Rabbit actually took a
watch out of its waistcoat-pocket, and looked at it, and then hurried
on, Alice started to her feet, for it flashed across her mind that she
had never before seen a rabbit with either a waistcoat-pocket, or a
watch to take out of it, and burning with curiosity, she ran across the
field after it, and fortunately was just in time to see it pop down a
large rabbit-hole under the hedge.

In another moment down went Alice after it, never once considering how
in the world she was to get out again.

The rabbit-hole went straight on like a tunnel for some way, and then
dipped suddenly down, so suddenly that Alice had not a moment to think
about stopping herself before she found herself falling down a very
deep well.
1-2.illustrationDesc: Alice B. Woodward ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Suddenly a white rabbit ran by
1-2.prevIllustration: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Chapter I title page ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice bored by the river
1-2.nextIllustration: William H. Walker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice falling

1-3.text:
Either the well was very deep, or she fell very slowly, for she had
plenty of time as she went down to look about her and to wonder what
was going to happen next. First, she tried to look down and make out
what she was coming to, but it was too dark to see anything; then she
looked at the sides of the well, and noticed that they were filled with
cupboards and book-shelves; here and there she saw maps and pictures
hung upon pegs. She took down a jar from one of the shelves as she
passed; it was labelled вҖңORANGE MARMALADEвҖқ, but to her great
disappointment it was empty: she did not like to drop the jar for fear
of killing somebody underneath, so managed to put it into one of the
cupboards as she fell past it.
1-3.illustrationDesc: William H. Walker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice falling
1-3.prevIllustration: Alice B. Woodward ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Suddenly a white rabbit ran by
1-3.nextIllustration: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice took marmalade as she fell

1-4.text:
вҖңWell!вҖқ thought Alice to herself, вҖңafter such a fall as this, I shall
think nothing of tumbling down stairs! How brave theyвҖҷll all think me
at home! Why, I wouldnвҖҷt say anything about it, even if I fell off the
top of the house!вҖқ (Which was very likely true.)

Down, down, down. Would the fall never come to an end? вҖңI wonder how
many miles IвҖҷve fallen by this time?вҖқ she said aloud. вҖңI must be
getting somewhere near the centre of the earth. Let me see: that would
be four thousand miles down, I thinkвҖ”вҖқ (for, you see, Alice had learnt
several things of this sort in her lessons in the schoolroom, and
though this was not a very good opportunity for showing off her
knowledge, as there was no one to listen to her, still it was good
practice to say it over) вҖңвҖ”yes, thatвҖҷs about the right distanceвҖ”but
then I wonder what Latitude or Longitude IвҖҷve got to?вҖқ (Alice had no
idea what Latitude was, or Longitude either, but thought they were nice
grand words to say.)
1-4.illustrationDesc: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice took marmalade as she fell
1-4.prevIllustration: William H. Walker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice falling
1-4.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice sitting with sister, beginning to get tired

1-5.text:
Presently she began again. вҖңI wonder if I shall fall right through
the earth! How funny itвҖҷll seem to come out among the people that walk
with their heads downward! The Antipathies, I thinkвҖ”вҖқ (she was rather
glad there was no one listening, this time, as it didnвҖҷt sound at all
the right word) вҖңвҖ”but I shall have to ask them what the name of the
country is, you know. Please, MaвҖҷam, is this New Zealand or Australia?вҖқ (and she tried to curtsey as she spokeвҖ”fancy curtseying as youвҖҷre
falling through the air! Do you think you could manage it?) вҖңAnd what
an ignorant little girl sheвҖҷll think me for asking! No, itвҖҷll never do
to ask: perhaps I shall see it written up somewhere.вҖқ

Down, down, down. There was nothing else to do, so Alice soon began
talking again. вҖңDinahвҖҷll miss me very much to-night, I should think!вҖқ (Dinah was the cat.) вҖңI hope theyвҖҷll remember her saucer of milk at
tea-time. Dinah my dear! I wish you were down here with me! There are
no mice in the air, IвҖҷm afraid, but you might catch a bat, and thatвҖҷs
very like a mouse, you know. But do cats eat bats, I wonder?вҖқ
1-5.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice sitting with sister, beginning to get tired
1-5.prevIllustration: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice took marmalade as she fell

1-6.text:
And here
Alice began to get rather sleepy, and went on saying to herself, in a
dreamy sort of way, вҖңDo cats eat bats? Do cats eat bats?вҖқ and
sometimes, вҖңDo bats eat cats?вҖқ for, you see, as she couldnвҖҷt answer
either question, it didnвҖҷt much matter which way she put it. She felt
that she was dozing off, and had just begun to dream that she was
walking hand in hand with Dinah, and saying to her very earnestly,
вҖңNow, Dinah, tell me the truth: did you ever eat a bat?вҖқ when suddenly,
thump! thump! down she came upon a heap of sticks and dry leaves, and
the fall was over.

Alice was not a bit hurt, and she jumped up on to her feet in a moment:
she looked up, but it was all dark overhead; before her was another
long passage, and the White Rabbit was still in sight, hurrying down
it. There was not a moment to be lost: away went Alice like the wind,
and was just in time to hear it say, as it turned a corner, вҖңOh my ears
and whiskers, how late itвҖҷs getting!вҖқ
1-6.prevIllustration: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice sitting with sister, beginning to get tired
1-6.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” White Rabbit running by with pocket watch

1-7.text:
She was close behind it when she
turned the corner, but the Rabbit was no longer to be seen: she found
herself in a long, low hall, which was lit up by a row of lamps hanging
from the roof.

There were doors all round the hall, but they were all locked; and when
Alice had been all the way down one side and up the other, trying every
door, she walked sadly down the middle, wondering how she was ever to
get out again.

Suddenly she came upon a little three-legged table, all made of solid
glass; there was nothing on it except a tiny golden key, and AliceвҖҷs
first thought was that it might belong to one of the doors of the hall;
but, alas! either the locks were too large, or the key was too small,
but at any rate it would not open any of them. However, on the second
time round, she came upon a low curtain she had not noticed before, and
behind it was a little door about fifteen inches high: she tried the
little golden key in the lock, and to her great delight it fitted!
1-7.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” White Rabbit running by with pocket watch
1-7.nextIllustration: DALL-E 3 (Rackham style) вҖ” The loveliest garden, seen through the tiny door",ilustrate this in a style comaptible with the images but maybe watercolor and wiht Alice looking a bit more like the real Alice and try to depict the images in the text of the apge next to it (asalways)",ilustrate this in a style comaptible with the images but maybe watercolor and wiht Alice looking a bit more like the real Alice and try to depict the images in the text of the apge next to it (asalways)

1-8.text:
Alice opened the door and found that it led into a small passage, not
much larger than a rat-hole: she knelt down and looked along the
passage into the loveliest garden you ever saw. How she longed to get
out of that dark hall, and wander about among those beds of bright
flowers and those cool fountains, but she could not even get her head
through the doorway; вҖңand even if my head would go through,вҖқ thought
poor Alice, вҖңit would be of very little use without my shoulders. Oh,
how I wish I could shut up like a telescope! I think I could, if I only
knew how to begin.вҖқ For, you see, so many out-of-the-way things had
happened lately, that Alice had begun to think that very few things
indeed were really impossible.
1-8.illustrationDesc: DALL-E 3 (Rackham style) вҖ” The loveliest garden, seen through the tiny door",ilustrate this in a style comaptible with the images but maybe watercolor and wiht Alice looking a bit more like the real Alice and try to depict the images in the text of the apge next to it (asalways)",ilustrate this in a style comaptible with the images but maybe watercolor and wiht Alice looking a bit more like the real Alice and try to depict the images in the text of the apge next to it (asalways)
1-8.prevIllustration: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” White Rabbit running by with pocket watch
1-8.nextIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” How Alice grew tall
1-8.note: ilustrate this in a style comaptible with the images but maybe watercolor and wiht Alice looking a bit more like the real Alice and try to depict the images in the text of the apge next to it (asalways)

1-9.text:
There seemed to be no use in waiting by the little door, so she went
back to the table, half hoping she might find another key on it, or at
any rate a book of rules for shutting people up like telescopes: this
time she found a little bottle on it, (вҖңwhich certainly was not here
before,вҖқ said Alice,) and round the neck of the bottle was a paper
label, with the words вҖңDRINK ME,вҖқ beautifully printed on it in large
letters.

It was all very well to say вҖңDrink me,вҖқ but the wise little Alice was
not going to do that in a hurry. вҖңNo, IвҖҷll look first,вҖқ she said,
вҖңand see whether itвҖҷs marked вҖҳpoisonвҖҷ or notвҖқ; for she had read
several nice little histories about children who had got burnt, and
eaten up by wild beasts and other unpleasant things, all because they
would not remember the simple rules their friends had taught them:
such as, that a red-hot poker will burn you if you hold it too long;
and that if you cut your finger very deeply with a knife, it usually
bleeds; and she had never forgotten that, if you drink much from a
bottle marked вҖңpoison,вҖқ it is almost certain to disagree with you,
sooner or later.
1-9.illustrationDesc: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” How Alice grew tall
1-9.prevIllustration: DALL-E 3 (Rackham style) вҖ” The loveliest garden, seen through the tiny door",ilustrate this in a style comaptible with the images but maybe watercolor and wiht Alice looking a bit more like the real Alice and try to depict the images in the text of the apge next to it (asalways)",ilustrate this in a style comaptible with the images but maybe watercolor and wiht Alice looking a bit more like the real Alice and try to depict the images in the text of the apge next to it (asalways)
1-9.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice falling down the rabbit-hole

1-10.text:
However, this bottle was not marked вҖңpoison,вҖқ so Alice ventured to
taste it, and finding it very nice, (it had, in fact, a sort of mixed
flavour of cherry-tart, custard, pine-apple, roast turkey, toffee, and
hot buttered toast,) she very soon finished it off.

вҖңWhat a curious feeling!вҖқ said Alice; вҖңI must be shutting up like a
telescope.вҖқ

And so it was indeed: she was now only ten inches high, and her face
brightened up at the thought that she was now the right size for going
through the little door into that lovely garden. First, however, she
waited for a few minutes to see if she was going to shrink any further:
she felt a little nervous about this; вҖңfor it might end, you know,вҖқ
said Alice to herself, вҖңin my going out altogether, like a candle. I
wonder what I should be like then?вҖқ And she tried to fancy what the
flame of a candle is like after the candle is blown out, for she could
not remember ever having seen such a thing.
1-10.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice falling down the rabbit-hole
1-10.prevIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” How Alice grew tall
1-10.nextIllustration: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Glass table

1-11.text:
After a while, finding that nothing more happened, she decided on going
into the garden at once; but, alas for poor Alice! when she got to the
door, she found she had forgotten the little golden key, and when she
went back to the table for it, she found she could not possibly reach
it: she could see it quite plainly through the glass, and she tried her
best to climb up one of the legs of the table, but it was too slippery;
and when she had tired herself out with trying, the poor little thing
sat down and cried. вҖңCome, thereвҖҷs no use in crying like that!вҖқ said Alice to herself,
rather sharply; вҖңI advise you to leave off this minute!вҖқ
1-11.illustrationDesc: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Glass table
1-11.prevIllustration: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice falling down the rabbit-hole
1-11.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll (photograph) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice Liddell portrait

1-12.text:
She generally
gave herself very good advice, (though she very seldom followed it),
and sometimes she scolded herself so severely as to bring tears into
her eyes; and once she remembered trying to box her own ears for having
cheated herself in a game of croquet she was playing against herself,
for this curious child was very fond of pretending to be two people. вҖңBut itвҖҷs no use now,вҖқ thought poor Alice, вҖңto pretend to be two
people! Why, thereвҖҷs hardly enough of me left to make one respectable
person!вҖқ Soon her eye fell on a little glass box that was lying under the table:
she opened it, and found in it a very small cake, on which the words
вҖңEAT MEвҖқ were beautifully marked in currants. вҖңWell, IвҖҷll eat it,вҖқ said
Alice, вҖңand if it makes me grow larger, I can reach the key; and if it
makes me grow smaller, I can creep under the door; so either way IвҖҷll
get into the garden, and I donвҖҷt care which happens!вҖқ
1-12.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll (photograph) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice Liddell portrait
1-12.prevIllustration: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Glass table

1-13.text:
She ate a little bit, and said anxiously to herself, вҖңWhich way? Which
way?вҖқ, holding her hand on the top of her head to feel which way it was
growing, and she was quite surprised to find that she remained the same
size: to be sure, this generally happens when one eats cake, but Alice
had got so much into the way of expecting nothing but out-of-the-way
things to happen, that it seemed quite dull and stupid for life to go
on in the common way. So she set to work, and very soon finished off the cake. *
1-13.prevIllustration: Lewis Carroll (photograph) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice Liddell portrait

2-1.text:
вҖңCuriouser and curiouser!вҖқ cried Alice (she was so much surprised, that
for the moment she quite forgot how to speak good English); вҖңnow IвҖҷm
opening out like the largest telescope that ever was! Good-bye, feet!вҖқ (for when she looked down at her feet, they seemed to be almost out of
sight, they were getting so far off). вҖңOh, my poor little feet, I
wonder who will put on your shoes and stockings for you now, dears? IвҖҷm
sure I shanвҖҷt be able! I shall be a great deal too far off to trouble
myself about you: you must manage the best way you can;вҖ”but I must be
kind to them,вҖқ thought Alice, вҖңor perhaps they wonвҖҷt walk the way I
want to go! Let me see: IвҖҷll give them a new pair of boots every
Christmas.вҖқ

And she went on planning to herself how she would manage it. вҖңThey must
go by the carrier,вҖқ she thought; вҖңand how funny itвҖҷll seem, sending
presents to oneвҖҷs own feet! And how odd the directions will look!
2-1.nextIllustration: DALL-E 3 (Rackham/Tenniel style) вҖў Nine feet tall, filling the room

2-2.text:
AliceвҖҷs Right Foot, Esq., Hearthrug, near the Fender, (with
     AliceвҖҷs love).

Oh dear, what nonsense IвҖҷm talking!вҖқ

Just then her head struck against the roof of the hall: in fact she was
now more than nine feet high, and she at once took up the little golden
key and hurried off to the garden door.

Poor Alice! It was as much as she could do, lying down on one side, to
look through into the garden with one eye; but to get through was more
hopeless than ever: she sat down and began to cry again.

вҖңYou ought to be ashamed of yourself,вҖқ said Alice, вҖңa great girl like
you,вҖқ (she might well say this), вҖңto go on crying in this way! Stop
this moment, I tell you!вҖқ But she went on all the same, shedding
gallons of tears, until there was a large pool all round her, about
four inches deep and reaching half down the hall.
2-2.illustrationDesc: DALL-E 3 (Rackham/Tenniel style) вҖў Nine feet tall, filling the room
2-2.note: ilustrate her head beating the roof

2-3.text:
After a time she heard a little pattering of feet in the distance, and
she hastily dried her eyes to see what was coming. It was the White
Rabbit returning, splendidly dressed, with a pair of white kid gloves
in one hand and a large fan in the other: he came trotting along in a
great hurry, muttering to himself as he came, вҖңOh! the Duchess, the
Duchess! Oh! wonвҖҷt she be savage if IвҖҷve kept her waiting!вҖқ Alice felt
so desperate that she was ready to ask help of any one; so, when the
Rabbit came near her, she began, in a low, timid voice, вҖңIf you please,
sirвҖ”вҖқ The Rabbit started violently, dropped the white kid gloves and
the fan, and skurried away into the darkness as hard as he could go.
2-3.prevIllustration: DALL-E 3 (Rackham/Tenniel style) вҖў Nine feet tall, filling the room

2-4.text:
Alice took up the fan and gloves, and, as the hall was very hot, she
kept fanning herself all the time she went on talking: вҖңDear, dear! How
queer everything is to-day! And yesterday things went on just as usual.
I wonder if IвҖҷve been changed in the night? Let me think: was I the
same when I got up this morning? I almost think I can remember feeling
a little different. But if IвҖҷm not the same, the next question is, Who
in the world am I? Ah, thatвҖҷs the great puzzle!вҖқ And she began
thinking over all the children she knew that were of the same age as
herself, to see if she could have been changed for any of them.

2-5.text:
вҖңIвҖҷm sure IвҖҷm not Ada,вҖқ she said, вҖңfor her hair goes in such long
ringlets, and mine doesnвҖҷt go in ringlets at all; and IвҖҷm sure I canвҖҷt
be Mabel, for I know all sorts of things, and she, oh! she knows such a
very little! Besides, sheвҖҷs she, and IвҖҷm I, andвҖ”oh dear, how
puzzling it all is! IвҖҷll try if I know all the things I used to know.
Let me see: four times five is twelve, and four times six is thirteen,
and four times seven isвҖ”oh dear! I shall never get to twenty at that
rate! However, the Multiplication Table doesnвҖҷt signify: letвҖҷs try
Geography. London is the capital of Paris, and Paris is the capital of
Rome, and RomeвҖ”no, thatвҖҷs all wrong, IвҖҷm certain! I must have been
changed for Mabel! IвҖҷll try and say вҖҳHow doth the littleвҖ”вҖҷвҖқ and she
crossed her hands on her lap as if she were saying lessons, and began
to repeat it, but her voice sounded hoarse and strange, and the words
did not come the same as they used to do:вҖ”

вҖңHow doth the little crocodile
    Improve his shining tail,
And pour the waters of the Nile
    On every golden scale!
2-5.nextIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice & Mouse in Pool of Tears

2-6.text:
вҖңHow cheerfully he seems to grin,
    How neatly spread his claws,
And welcome little fishes in
    With gently smiling jaws!вҖқ

вҖңIвҖҷm sure those are not the right words,вҖқ said poor Alice, and her eyes
filled with tears again as she went on, вҖңI must be Mabel after all, and
I shall have to go and live in that poky little house, and have next to
no toys to play with, and oh! ever so many lessons to learn! No, IвҖҷve
made up my mind about it; if IвҖҷm Mabel, IвҖҷll stay down here! ItвҖҷll be
no use their putting their heads down and saying вҖҳCome up again, dear!вҖҷ
I shall only look up and say вҖҳWho am I then? Tell me that first, and
then, if I like being that person, IвҖҷll come up: if not, IвҖҷll stay down
here till IвҖҷm somebody elseвҖҷвҖ”but, oh dear!вҖқ cried Alice, with a sudden
burst of tears, вҖңI do wish they would put their heads down! I am so
very tired of being all alone here!вҖқ
2-6.illustrationDesc: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice & Mouse in Pool of Tears
2-6.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice and the Mouse swimming in the pool of tears

2-7.text:
As she said this she looked down at her hands, and was surprised to see
that she had put on one of the RabbitвҖҷs little white kid gloves while
she was talking. вҖңHow can I have done that?вҖқ she thought. вҖңI must be
growing small again.вҖқ She got up and went to the table to measure
herself by it, and found that, as nearly as she could guess, she was
now about two feet high, and was going on shrinking rapidly: she soon
found out that the cause of this was the fan she was holding, and she
dropped it hastily, just in time to avoid shrinking away altogether.
2-7.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice and the Mouse swimming in the pool of tears
2-7.prevIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice & Mouse in Pool of Tears

2-8.text:
вҖңThat was a narrow escape!вҖқ said Alice, a good deal frightened at the
sudden change, but very glad to find herself still in existence; вҖңand
now for the garden!вҖқ and she ran with all speed back to the little
door: but, alas! the little door was shut again, and the little golden
key was lying on the glass table as before, вҖңand things are worse than
ever,вҖқ thought the poor child, вҖңfor I never was so small as this
before, never! And I declare itвҖҷs too bad, that it is!вҖқ

As she said these words her foot slipped, and in another moment,
splash! she was up to her chin in salt water. Her first idea was that
she had somehow fallen into the sea, вҖңand in that case I can go back by
railway,вҖқ she said to herself. (Alice had been to the seaside once in
her life, and had come to the general conclusion, that wherever you go
to on the English coast you find a number of bathing machines in the
sea, some children digging in the sand with wooden spades, then a row
of lodging houses, and behind them a railway station.)
2-8.prevIllustration: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice and the Mouse swimming in the pool of tears
2-8.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice drinking from the DRINK ME bottle

2-9.text:
However, she
soon made out that she was in the pool of tears which she had wept when
she was nine feet high.

вҖңI wish I hadnвҖҷt cried so much!вҖқ said Alice, as she swam about, trying
to find her way out. вҖңI shall be punished for it now, I suppose, by
being drowned in my own tears! That will be a queer thing, to be
sure! However, everything is queer to-day.вҖқ

Just then she heard something splashing about in the pool a little way
off, and she swam nearer to make out what it was: at first she thought
it must be a walrus or hippopotamus, but then she remembered how small
she was now, and she soon made out that it was only a mouse that had
slipped in like herself.

вҖңWould it be of any use, now,вҖқ thought Alice, вҖңto speak to this mouse?
Everything is so out-of-the-way down here, that I should think very
likely it can talk: at any rate, thereвҖҷs no harm in trying.вҖқ So she
began: вҖңO Mouse, do you know the way out of this pool? I am very tired
of swimming about here, O Mouse!вҖқ
2-9.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice drinking from the DRINK ME bottle
2-9.nextIllustration: Alice B. Woodward ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” They all swam to the shore

2-10.text:
(Alice thought this must be the right
way of speaking to a mouse: she had never done such a thing before, but
she remembered having seen in her brotherвҖҷs Latin Grammar, вҖңA mouseвҖ”of
a mouseвҖ”to a mouseвҖ”a mouseвҖ”O mouse!вҖқ) The Mouse looked at her rather
inquisitively, and seemed to her to wink with one of its little eyes,
but it said nothing.

вҖңPerhaps it doesnвҖҷt understand English,вҖқ thought Alice; вҖңI daresay itвҖҷs
a French mouse, come over with William the Conqueror.вҖқ (For, with all
her knowledge of history, Alice had no very clear notion how long ago
anything had happened.) So she began again: вҖңOГ№ est ma chatte?вҖқ which
was the first sentence in her French lesson-book. The Mouse gave a
sudden leap out of the water, and seemed to quiver all over with
fright. вҖңOh, I beg your pardon!вҖқ cried Alice hastily, afraid that she
had hurt the poor animalвҖҷs feelings. вҖңI quite forgot you didnвҖҷt like
cats.вҖқ
2-10.illustrationDesc: Alice B. Woodward ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” They all swam to the shore
2-10.prevIllustration: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice drinking from the DRINK ME bottle
2-10.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Carroll manuscript Chapter II - Caucus Race party

2-11.text:
вҖңNot like cats!вҖқ cried the Mouse, in a shrill, passionate voice. вҖңWould
you like cats if you were me?вҖқ вҖңWell, perhaps not,вҖқ said Alice in a soothing tone: вҖңdonвҖҷt be angry
about it. And yet I wish I could show you our cat Dinah: I think youвҖҷd
take a fancy to cats if you could only see her. She is such a dear
quiet thing,вҖқ Alice went on, half to herself, as she swam lazily about
in the pool, вҖңand she sits purring so nicely by the fire, licking her
paws and washing her faceвҖ”and she is such a nice soft thing to
nurseвҖ”and sheвҖҷs such a capital one for catching miceвҖ”oh, I beg your
pardon!вҖқ cried Alice again, for this time the Mouse was bristling all
over, and she felt certain it must be really offended. вҖңWe wonвҖҷt talk
about her any more if youвҖҷd rather not.вҖқ вҖңWe indeed!вҖқ cried the Mouse, who was trembling down to the end of his
tail. вҖңAs if I would talk on such a subject! Our family always
hated cats: nasty, low, vulgar things! DonвҖҷt let me hear the name
again!вҖқ
2-11.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Carroll manuscript Chapter II - Caucus Race party
2-11.prevIllustration: Alice B. Woodward ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” They all swam to the shore
2-11.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice swimming with the Mouse

2-12.text:
вҖңI wonвҖҷt indeed!вҖқ said Alice, in a great hurry to change the subject of
conversation. вҖңAre youвҖ”are you fondвҖ”ofвҖ”of dogs?вҖқ The Mouse did not
answer, so Alice went on eagerly: вҖңThere is such a nice little dog near
our house I should like to show you! A little bright-eyed terrier, you
know, with oh, such long curly brown hair! And itвҖҷll fetch things when
you throw them, and itвҖҷll sit up and beg for its dinner, and all sorts
of thingsвҖ”I canвҖҷt remember half of themвҖ”and it belongs to a farmer, you
know, and he says itвҖҷs so useful, itвҖҷs worth a hundred pounds! He says
it kills all the rats andвҖ”oh dear!вҖқ cried Alice in a sorrowful tone,
вҖңIвҖҷm afraid IвҖҷve offended it again!вҖқ For the Mouse was swimming away
from her as hard as it could go, and making quite a commotion in the
pool as it went. So she called softly after it, вҖңMouse dear! Do come back again, and we
wonвҖҷt talk about cats or dogs either, if you donвҖҷt like them!вҖқ
2-12.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice swimming with the Mouse
2-12.prevIllustration: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Carroll manuscript Chapter II - Caucus Race party
2-12.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Carroll manuscript Chapter III - Alice and the puppy

2-13.text:
When the
Mouse heard this, it turned round and swam slowly back to her: its face
was quite pale (with passion, Alice thought), and it said in a low
trembling voice, вҖңLet us get to the shore, and then IвҖҷll tell you my
history, and youвҖҷll understand why it is I hate cats and dogs.вҖқ It was high time to go, for the pool was getting quite crowded with the
birds and animals that had fallen into it: there were a Duck and a
Dodo, a Lory and an Eaglet, and several other curious creatures. Alice
led the way, and the whole party swam to the shore.
2-13.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Carroll manuscript Chapter III - Alice and the puppy
2-13.prevIllustration: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice swimming with the Mouse

3-1.chapterName: A Caucus-Race and a Long Tale
3-1.text:
They were indeed a queer-looking party that assembled on the bankвҖ”the
birds with draggled feathers, the animals with their fur clinging close
to them, and all dripping wet, cross, and uncomfortable.

The first question of course was, how to get dry again: they had a
consultation about this, and after a few minutes it seemed quite
natural to Alice to find herself talking familiarly with them, as if
she had known them all her life. Indeed, she had quite a long argument
with the Lory, who at last turned sulky, and would only say, вҖңI am
older than you, and must know better;вҖқ and this Alice would not allow
without knowing how old it was, and, as the Lory positively refused to
tell its age, there was no more to be said.

At last the Mouse, who seemed to be a person of authority among them,
called out, вҖңSit down, all of you, and listen to me! IвҖҷll soon make
you dry enough!вҖқ They all sat down at once, in a large ring, with the
Mouse in the middle. Alice kept her eyes anxiously fixed on it, for she
felt sure she would catch a bad cold if she did not get dry very soon.
3-1.illustrationDesc: Bessie Pease Gutmann ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Beg your acceptance
3-1.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Dodo presenting Alice with her own thimble as prize

3-2.chapterName: A Caucus-Race and a Long Tale
3-2.text:
вҖңAhem!вҖқ said the Mouse with an important air, вҖңare you all ready? This
is the driest thing I know. Silence all round, if you please! вҖҳWilliam
the Conqueror, whose cause was favoured by the pope, was soon submitted
to by the English, who wanted leaders, and had been of late much
accustomed to usurpation and conquest. Edwin and Morcar, the earls of
Mercia and NorthumbriaвҖ”вҖҷвҖқ

вҖңUgh!вҖқ said the Lory, with a shiver.

вҖңI beg your pardon!вҖқ said the Mouse, frowning, but very politely: вҖңDid
you speak?вҖқ

вҖңNot I!вҖқ said the Lory hastily.

вҖңI thought you did,вҖқ said the Mouse. вҖңвҖ”I proceed. вҖҳEdwin and Morcar,
the earls of Mercia and Northumbria, declared for him: and even
Stigand, the patriotic archbishop of Canterbury, found it advisableвҖ”вҖҷвҖқ

вҖңFound what?вҖқ said the Duck.

вҖңFound it,вҖқ the Mouse replied rather crossly: вҖңof course you know
what вҖҳitвҖҷ means.вҖқ
3-2.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Dodo presenting Alice with her own thimble as prize
3-2.prevIllustration: Bessie Pease Gutmann ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Beg your acceptance
3-2.nextIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Hand it over said Dodo

3-3.chapterName: A Caucus-Race and a Long Tale
3-3.text:
вҖңI know what вҖҳitвҖҷ means well enough, when I find a thing,вҖқ said the
Duck: вҖңitвҖҷs generally a frog or a worm. The question is, what did the
archbishop find?вҖқ

The Mouse did not notice this question, but hurriedly went on, вҖңвҖҳвҖ”found
it advisable to go with Edgar Atheling to meet William and offer him
the crown. WilliamвҖҷs conduct at first was moderate. But the insolence
of his NormansвҖ”вҖҷ How are you getting on now, my dear?вҖқ it continued,
turning to Alice as it spoke.

вҖңAs wet as ever,вҖқ said Alice in a melancholy tone: вҖңit doesnвҖҷt seem to
dry me at all.вҖқ

вҖңIn that case,вҖқ said the Dodo solemnly, rising to its feet, вҖңI move
that the meeting adjourn, for the immediate adoption of more energetic
remediesвҖ”вҖқ

вҖңSpeak English!вҖқ said the Eaglet. вҖңI donвҖҷt know the meaning of half
those long words, and, whatвҖҷs more, I donвҖҷt believe you do either!вҖқ And
the Eaglet bent down its head to hide a smile: some of the other birds
tittered audibly.
3-3.illustrationDesc: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Hand it over said Dodo
3-3.prevIllustration: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Dodo presenting Alice with her own thimble as prize

3-4.chapterName: A Caucus-Race and a Long Tale
3-4.text:
вҖңWhat I was going to say,вҖқ said the Dodo in an offended tone, вҖңwas,
that the best thing to get us dry would be a Caucus-race.вҖқ

вҖңWhat is a Caucus-race?вҖқ said Alice; not that she wanted much to
know, but the Dodo had paused as if it thought that somebody ought to
speak, and no one else seemed inclined to say anything.

вҖңWhy,вҖқ said the Dodo, вҖңthe best way to explain it is to do it.вҖқ (And,
as you might like to try the thing yourself, some winter day, I will
tell you how the Dodo managed it.)

First it marked out a race-course, in a sort of circle, (вҖңthe exact
shape doesnвҖҷt matter,вҖқ it said,) and then all the party were placed
along the course, here and there. There was no вҖңOne, two, three, and
away,вҖқ but they began running when they liked, and left off when they
liked, so that it was not easy to know when the race was over. However,
when they had been running half an hour or so, and were quite dry
again, the Dodo suddenly called out вҖңThe race is over!вҖқ and they all
crowded round it, panting, and asking, вҖңBut who has won?вҖқ
3-4.prevIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Hand it over said Dodo
3-4.nextIllustration: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Dodo presents Alice with her fingernail

3-5.chapterName: A Caucus-Race and a Long Tale
3-5.text:
This question the Dodo could not answer without a great deal of
thought, and it sat for a long time with one finger pressed upon its
forehead (the position in which you usually see Shakespeare, in the
pictures of him), while the rest waited in silence. At last the Dodo
said, вҖңEverybody has won, and all must have prizes.вҖқ

вҖңBut who is to give the prizes?вҖқ quite a chorus of voices asked.

вҖңWhy, she, of course,вҖқ said the Dodo, pointing to Alice with one
finger; and the whole party at once crowded round her, calling out in a
confused way, вҖңPrizes! Prizes!вҖқ

Alice had no idea what to do, and in despair she put her hand in her
pocket, and pulled out a box of comfits, (luckily the salt water had
not got into it), and handed them round as prizes. There was exactly
one a-piece, all round.

вҖңBut she must have a prize herself, you know,вҖқ said the Mouse.

вҖңOf course,вҖқ the Dodo replied very gravely. вҖңWhat else have you got in
your pocket?вҖқ he went on, turning to Alice.
3-5.illustrationDesc: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Dodo presents Alice with her fingernail
3-5.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice distributing comfits to the animals

3-6.chapterName: A Caucus-Race and a Long Tale
3-6.text:
вҖңOnly a thimble,вҖқ said Alice sadly.

вҖңHand it over here,вҖқ said the Dodo.

Then they all crowded round her once more, while the Dodo solemnly
presented the thimble, saying вҖңWe beg your acceptance of this elegant
thimble;вҖқ and, when it had finished this short speech, they all
cheered.

Alice thought the whole thing very absurd, but they all looked so grave
that she did not dare to laugh; and, as she could not think of anything
to say, she simply bowed, and took the thimble, looking as solemn as
she could.

The next thing was to eat the comfits: this caused some noise and
confusion, as the large birds complained that they could not taste
theirs, and the small ones choked and had to be patted on the back. However, it was over at last, and they sat down again in a ring, and
begged the Mouse to tell them something more.
3-6.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice distributing comfits to the animals
3-6.prevIllustration: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Dodo presents Alice with her fingernail
3-6.nextIllustration: John Tenniel & Harry Theaker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Dodo presents thimble to Alice

3-7.chapterName: A Caucus-Race and a Long Tale
3-7.text:
вҖңYou promised to tell me your history, you know,вҖқ said Alice, вҖңand why
it is you hateвҖ”C and D,вҖқ she added in a whisper, half afraid that it
would be offended again.

вҖңMine is a long and a sad tale!вҖқ said the Mouse, turning to Alice, and
sighing.

вҖңIt is a long tail, certainly,вҖқ said Alice, looking down with wonder
at the MouseвҖҷs tail; вҖңbut why do you call it sad?вҖқ And she kept on
puzzling about it while the Mouse was speaking, so that her idea of the
tale was something like this:вҖ”

         вҖңFury said to a mouse, That he met in the house, вҖҳLet us both
         go to law: I will prosecute you.вҖ”Come, IвҖҷll take no
         denial; We must have a trial: For really this morning IвҖҷve
         nothing to do.вҖҷ Said the mouse to the cur, вҖҳSuch a trial, dear
         sir, With no jury or judge, would be wasting our breath.вҖҷ
         вҖҳIвҖҷll be judge, IвҖҷll be jury,вҖҷ Said cunning old Fury: вҖҳIвҖҷll
         try the whole cause, and condemn you to death.вҖҷвҖқ
3-7.illustrationDesc: John Tenniel & Harry Theaker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Dodo presents thimble to Alice
3-7.prevIllustration: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice distributing comfits to the animals
3-7.nextIllustration: Arthur Rackham ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Who has won

3-8.chapterName: A Caucus-Race and a Long Tale
3-8.text:
вҖңYou are not attending!вҖқ said the Mouse to Alice severely. вҖңWhat are
you thinking of?вҖқ

вҖңI beg your pardon,вҖқ said Alice very humbly: вҖңyou had got to the fifth
bend, I think?вҖқ

вҖңI had not!вҖқ cried the Mouse, sharply and very angrily.

вҖңA knot!вҖқ said Alice, always ready to make herself useful, and looking
anxiously about her. вҖңOh, do let me help to undo it!вҖқ

вҖңI shall do nothing of the sort,вҖқ said the Mouse, getting up and
walking away. вҖңYou insult me by talking such nonsense!вҖқ

вҖңI didnвҖҷt mean it!вҖқ pleaded poor Alice. вҖңBut youвҖҷre so easily offended,
you know!вҖқ

The Mouse only growled in reply.

вҖңPlease come back and finish your story!вҖқ Alice called after it; and
the others all joined in chorus, вҖңYes, please do!вҖқ but the Mouse only
shook its head impatiently, and walked a little quicker.
3-8.illustrationDesc: Arthur Rackham ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Who has won
3-8.prevIllustration: John Tenniel & Harry Theaker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Dodo presents thimble to Alice
3-8.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Mouse tells its history ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” the driest thing

3-9.chapterName: A Caucus-Race and a Long Tale
3-9.text:
вҖңWhat a pity it wouldnвҖҷt stay!вҖқ sighed the Lory, as soon as it was
quite out of sight; and an old Crab took the opportunity of saying to
her daughter вҖңAh, my dear! Let this be a lesson to you never to lose
your temper!вҖқ вҖңHold your tongue, Ma!вҖқ said the young Crab, a little
snappishly. вҖңYouвҖҷre enough to try the patience of an oyster!вҖқ вҖңI wish I had our Dinah here, I know I do!вҖқ said Alice aloud,
addressing nobody in particular. вҖңSheвҖҷd soon fetch it back!вҖқ вҖңAnd who is Dinah, if I might venture to ask the question?вҖқ said the
Lory. Alice replied eagerly, for she was always ready to talk about her pet:
вҖңDinahвҖҷs our cat. And sheвҖҷs such a capital one for catching mice you
canвҖҷt think! And oh, I wish you could see her after the birds! Why,
sheвҖҷll eat a little bird as soon as look at it!вҖқ This speech caused a remarkable sensation among the party.
3-9.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Mouse tells its history ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” the driest thing
3-9.prevIllustration: Arthur Rackham ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Who has won
3-9.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll (photograph) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice in feigned sleep, 1860

3-10.chapterName: A Caucus-Race and a Long Tale
3-10.text:
Some of the
birds hurried off at once: one old Magpie began wrapping itself up very
carefully, remarking, вҖңI really must be getting home; the night-air
doesnвҖҷt suit my throat!вҖқ and a Canary called out in a trembling voice
to its children, вҖңCome away, my dears! ItвҖҷs high time you were all in
bed!вҖқ On various pretexts they all moved off, and Alice was soon left
alone. вҖңI wish I hadnвҖҷt mentioned Dinah!вҖқ she said to herself in a melancholy
tone. вҖңNobody seems to like her, down here, and IвҖҷm sure sheвҖҷs the best
cat in the world! Oh, my dear Dinah! I wonder if I shall ever see you
any more!вҖқ And here poor Alice began to cry again, for she felt very
lonely and low-spirited. In a little while, however, she again heard a
little pattering of footsteps in the distance, and she looked up
eagerly, half hoping that the Mouse had changed his mind, and was
coming back to finish his story.
3-10.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll (photograph) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice in feigned sleep, 1860
3-10.prevIllustration: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Mouse tells its history ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” the driest thing

4-1.chapterName: The Rabbit Sends in a Little Bill
4-1.text:
It was the White Rabbit, trotting slowly back again, and looking
anxiously about as it went, as if it had lost something; and she heard
it muttering to itself вҖңThe Duchess! The Duchess! Oh my dear paws! Oh
my fur and whiskers! SheвҖҷll get me executed, as sure as ferrets are
ferrets! Where can I have dropped them, I wonder?вҖқ Alice guessed in a
moment that it was looking for the fan and the pair of white kid
gloves, and she very good-naturedly began hunting about for them, but
they were nowhere to be seenвҖ”everything seemed to have changed since
her swim in the pool, and the great hall, with the glass table and the
little door, had vanished completely.
4-1.illustrationDesc: Bessie Pease Gutmann ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Hope I shan't grow more
4-1.nextIllustration: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” White rabbit falls

4-2.chapterName: The Rabbit Sends in a Little Bill
4-2.text:
Very soon the Rabbit noticed Alice, as she went hunting about, and
called out to her in an angry tone, вҖңWhy, Mary Ann, what are you
doing out here? Run home this moment, and fetch me a pair of gloves and
a fan! Quick, now!вҖқ And Alice was so much frightened that she ran off
at once in the direction it pointed to, without trying to explain the
mistake it had made.

вҖңHe took me for his housemaid,вҖқ she said to herself as she ran. вҖңHow
surprised heвҖҷll be when he finds out who I am! But IвҖҷd better take him
his fan and glovesвҖ”that is, if I can find them.вҖқ As she said this, she
came upon a neat little house, on the door of which was a bright brass
plate with the name вҖңW. RABBIT,вҖқ engraved upon it. She went in without
knocking, and hurried upstairs, in great fear lest she should meet the
real Mary Ann, and be turned out of the house before she had found the
fan and gloves.
4-2.illustrationDesc: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” White rabbit falls
4-2.prevIllustration: Bessie Pease Gutmann ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Hope I shan't grow more
4-2.nextIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” She grew and grew

4-3.chapterName: The Rabbit Sends in a Little Bill
4-3.text:
вҖңHow queer it seems,вҖқ Alice said to herself, вҖңto be going messages for
a rabbit! I suppose DinahвҖҷll be sending me on messages next!вҖқ And she
began fancying the sort of thing that would happen: вҖңвҖҳMiss Alice! Come
here directly, and get ready for your walk!вҖҷ вҖҳComing in a minute,
nurse! But IвҖҷve got to see that the mouse doesnвҖҷt get out.вҖҷ Only I
donвҖҷt think,вҖқ Alice went on, вҖңthat theyвҖҷd let Dinah stop in the house
if it began ordering people about like that!вҖқ

By this time she had found her way into a tidy little room with a table
in the window, and on it (as she had hoped) a fan and two or three
pairs of tiny white kid gloves: she took up the fan and a pair of the
gloves, and was just going to leave the room, when her eye fell upon a
little bottle that stood near the looking-glass. There was no label
this time with the words вҖңDRINK ME,вҖқ but nevertheless she uncorked it
and put it to her lips.
4-3.illustrationDesc: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” She grew and grew
4-3.prevIllustration: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” White rabbit falls
4-3.nextIllustration: Harry Rountree ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Why, Mary Ann

4-4.chapterName: The Rabbit Sends in a Little Bill
4-4.text:
вҖңI know something interesting is sure to
happen,вҖқ she said to herself, вҖңwhenever I eat or drink anything; so
IвҖҷll just see what this bottle does. I do hope itвҖҷll make me grow large
again, for really IвҖҷm quite tired of being such a tiny little thing!вҖқ

It did so indeed, and much sooner than she had expected: before she had
drunk half the bottle, she found her head pressing against the ceiling,
and had to stoop to save her neck from being broken. She hastily put
down the bottle, saying to herself вҖңThatвҖҷs quite enoughвҖ”I hope I shanвҖҷt
grow any moreвҖ”As it is, I canвҖҷt get out at the doorвҖ”I do wish I hadnвҖҷt
drunk quite so much!вҖқ

Alas! it was too late to wish that! She went on growing, and growing,
and very soon had to kneel down on the floor: in another minute there
was not even room for this, and she tried the effect of lying down with
one elbow against the door, and the other arm curled round her head.
4-4.illustrationDesc: Harry Rountree ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Why, Mary Ann
4-4.prevIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” She grew and grew
4-4.nextIllustration: John Tenniel & Harry Theaker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Rabbit started violently

4-5.chapterName: The Rabbit Sends in a Little Bill
4-5.text:
Still she went on growing, and, as a last resource, she put one arm out
of the window, and one foot up the chimney, and said to herself вҖңNow I
can do no more, whatever happens. What will become of me?вҖқ

Luckily for Alice, the little magic bottle had now had its full effect,
and she grew no larger: still it was very uncomfortable, and, as there
seemed to be no sort of chance of her ever getting out of the room
again, no wonder she felt unhappy.

вҖңIt was much pleasanter at home,вҖқ thought poor Alice, вҖңwhen one wasnвҖҷt
always growing larger and smaller, and being ordered about by mice and
rabbits. I almost wish I hadnвҖҷt gone down that rabbit-holeвҖ”and yetвҖ”and
yetвҖ”itвҖҷs rather curious, you know, this sort of life! I do wonder what
can have happened to me! When I used to read fairy-tales, I fancied
that kind of thing never happened, and now here I am in the middle of
one! There ought to be a book written about me, that there ought! And
when I grow up, IвҖҷll write oneвҖ”but IвҖҷm grown up now,вҖқ she added in a
sorrowful tone; вҖңat least thereвҖҷs no room to grow up any more here.вҖқ
4-5.illustrationDesc: John Tenniel & Harry Theaker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Rabbit started violently
4-5.prevIllustration: Harry Rountree ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Why, Mary Ann
4-5.nextIllustration: Gwynedd Hudson ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Poor little lizard

4-6.chapterName: The Rabbit Sends in a Little Bill
4-6.text:
вҖңBut then,вҖқ thought Alice, вҖңshall I never get any older than I am
now? ThatвҖҷll be a comfort, one wayвҖ”never to be an old womanвҖ”but
thenвҖ”always to have lessons to learn! Oh, I shouldnвҖҷt like that!вҖқ

вҖңOh, you foolish Alice!вҖқ she answered herself. вҖңHow can you learn
lessons in here? Why, thereвҖҷs hardly room for you, and no room at all
for any lesson-books!вҖқ

And so she went on, taking first one side and then the other, and
making quite a conversation of it altogether; but after a few minutes
she heard a voice outside, and stopped to listen.

вҖңMary Ann! Mary Ann!вҖқ said the voice. вҖңFetch me my gloves this moment!вҖқ Then came a little pattering of feet on the stairs. Alice knew it was
the Rabbit coming to look for her, and she trembled till she shook the
house, quite forgetting that she was now about a thousand times as
large as the Rabbit, and had no reason to be afraid of it.
4-6.illustrationDesc: Gwynedd Hudson ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Poor little lizard
4-6.prevIllustration: John Tenniel & Harry Theaker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Rabbit started violently
4-6.nextIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Bill the Lizard

4-7.chapterName: The Rabbit Sends in a Little Bill
4-7.text:
Presently the Rabbit came up to the door, and tried to open it; but, as
the door opened inwards, and AliceвҖҷs elbow was pressed hard against it,
that attempt proved a failure. Alice heard it say to itself вҖңThen IвҖҷll
go round and get in at the window.вҖқ

вҖңThat you wonвҖҷt!вҖқ thought Alice, and, after waiting till she fancied
she heard the Rabbit just under the window, she suddenly spread out her
hand, and made a snatch in the air. She did not get hold of anything,
but she heard a little shriek and a fall, and a crash of broken glass,
from which she concluded that it was just possible it had fallen into a
cucumber-frame, or something of the sort.

Next came an angry voiceвҖ”the RabbitвҖҷsвҖ”вҖңPat! Pat! Where are you?вҖқ And
then a voice she had never heard before, вҖңSure then IвҖҷm here! Digging
for apples, yer honour!вҖқ

вҖңDigging for apples, indeed!вҖқ said the Rabbit angrily. вҖңHere! Come and
help me out of this!вҖқ (Sounds of more broken glass.)
4-7.illustrationDesc: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Bill the Lizard
4-7.prevIllustration: Gwynedd Hudson ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Poor little lizard
4-7.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Bill the Lizard being kicked up the chimney

4-8.chapterName: The Rabbit Sends in a Little Bill
4-8.text:
вҖңNow tell me, Pat, whatвҖҷs that in the window?вҖқ

вҖңSure, itвҖҷs an arm, yer honour!вҖқ (He pronounced it вҖңarrum.вҖқ)

вҖңAn arm, you goose! Who ever saw one that size? Why, it fills the whole
window!вҖқ

вҖңSure, it does, yer honour: but itвҖҷs an arm for all that.вҖқ

вҖңWell, itвҖҷs got no business there, at any rate: go and take it away!вҖқ

There was a long silence after this, and Alice could only hear whispers
now and then; such as, вҖңSure, I donвҖҷt like it, yer honour, at all, at
all!вҖқ вҖңDo as I tell you, you coward!вҖқ and at last she spread out her
hand again, and made another snatch in the air. This time there were
two little shrieks, and more sounds of broken glass. вҖңWhat a number
of cucumber-frames there must be!вҖқ thought Alice. вҖңI wonder what
theyвҖҷll do next! As for pulling me out of the window, I only wish they
could! IвҖҷm sure I donвҖҷt want to stay in here any longer!вҖқ
4-8.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Bill the Lizard being kicked up the chimney
4-8.prevIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Bill the Lizard
4-8.nextIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Dash the Puppy

4-9.chapterName: The Rabbit Sends in a Little Bill
4-9.text:
She waited for some time without hearing anything more: at last came a
rumbling of little cartwheels, and the sound of a good many voices all
talking together: she made out the words: вҖңWhereвҖҷs the other
ladder?вҖ”Why, I hadnвҖҷt to bring but one; BillвҖҷs got the otherвҖ”Bill!
fetch it here, lad!вҖ”Here, put вҖҷem up at this cornerвҖ”No, tie вҖҷem
together firstвҖ”they donвҖҷt reach half high enough yetвҖ”Oh! theyвҖҷll do
well enough; donвҖҷt be particularвҖ”Here, Bill! catch hold of this
ropeвҖ”Will the roof bear?вҖ”Mind that loose slateвҖ”Oh, itвҖҷs coming down!
Heads below!вҖқ (a loud crash)вҖ”вҖңNow, who did that?вҖ”It was Bill, I
fancyвҖ”WhoвҖҷs to go down the chimney?вҖ”Nay, I shanвҖҷt! You do
it!вҖ”That I wonвҖҷt, then!вҖ”BillвҖҷs to go downвҖ”Here, Bill! the master says
youвҖҷre to go down the chimney!вҖқ

вҖңOh! So BillвҖҷs got to come down the chimney, has he?вҖқ said Alice to
herself. вҖңShy, they seem to put everything upon Bill! I wouldnвҖҷt be in
BillвҖҷs place for a good deal: this fireplace is narrow, to be sure; but
I think I can kick a little!вҖқ
4-9.illustrationDesc: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Dash the Puppy
4-9.prevIllustration: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Bill the Lizard being kicked up the chimney
4-9.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice alone on the shore after the race

4-10.chapterName: The Rabbit Sends in a Little Bill
4-10.text:
She drew her foot as far down the chimney as she could, and waited till
she heard a little animal (she couldnвҖҷt guess of what sort it was)
scratching and scrambling about in the chimney close above her: then,
saying to herself вҖңThis is Bill,вҖқ she gave one sharp kick, and waited
to see what would happen next.

The first thing she heard was a general chorus of вҖңThere goes Bill!вҖқ
then the RabbitвҖҷs voice alongвҖ”вҖңCatch him, you by the hedge!вҖқ then
silence, and then another confusion of voicesвҖ”вҖңHold up his headвҖ”Brandy
nowвҖ”DonвҖҷt choke himвҖ”How was it, old fellow? What happened to you? Tell
us all about it!вҖқ

Last came a little feeble, squeaking voice, (вҖңThatвҖҷs Bill,вҖқ thought
Alice,) вҖңWell, I hardly knowвҖ”No more, thank ye; IвҖҷm better nowвҖ”but IвҖҷm
a deal too flustered to tell youвҖ”all I know is, something comes at me
like a Jack-in-the-box, and up I goes like a sky-rocket!вҖқ
4-10.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice alone on the shore after the race
4-10.prevIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Dash the Puppy
4-10.nextIllustration: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” White rabbit falls

4-11.chapterName: The Rabbit Sends in a Little Bill
4-11.text:
вҖңSo you did, old fellow!вҖқ said the others.

вҖңWe must burn the house down!вҖқ said the RabbitвҖҷs voice; and Alice
called out as loud as she could, вҖңIf you do, IвҖҷll set Dinah at you!вҖқ

There was a dead silence instantly, and Alice thought to herself, вҖңI
wonder what they will do next! If they had any sense, theyвҖҷd take the
roof off.вҖқ After a minute or two, they began moving about again, and
Alice heard the Rabbit say, вҖңA barrowful will do, to begin with.вҖқ

вҖңA barrowful of what?вҖқ thought Alice; but she had not long to doubt,
for the next moment a shower of little pebbles came rattling in at the
window, and some of them hit her in the face. вҖңIвҖҷll put a stop to
this,вҖқ she said to herself, and shouted out, вҖңYouвҖҷd better not do that
again!вҖқ which produced another dead silence.
4-11.illustrationDesc: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” White rabbit falls
4-11.prevIllustration: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice alone on the shore after the race
4-11.nextIllustration: Harry Rountree ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Poor Lizard Bill

4-12.chapterName: The Rabbit Sends in a Little Bill
4-12.text:
Alice noticed with some surprise that the pebbles were all turning into
little cakes as they lay on the floor, and a bright idea came into her
head. вҖңIf I eat one of these cakes,вҖқ she thought, вҖңitвҖҷs sure to make
some change in my size; and as it canвҖҷt possibly make me larger, it
must make me smaller, I suppose.вҖқ

So she swallowed one of the cakes, and was delighted to find that she
began shrinking directly. As soon as she was small enough to get
through the door, she ran out of the house, and found quite a crowd of
little animals and birds waiting outside. The poor little Lizard, Bill,
was in the middle, being held up by two guinea-pigs, who were giving it
something out of a bottle. They all made a rush at Alice the moment she
appeared; but she ran off as hard as she could, and soon found herself
safe in a thick wood.
4-12.illustrationDesc: Harry Rountree ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Poor Lizard Bill
4-12.prevIllustration: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” White rabbit falls
4-12.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” White Rabbit ordering Alice about

4-13.chapterName: The Rabbit Sends in a Little Bill
4-13.text:
вҖңThe first thing IвҖҷve got to do,вҖқ said Alice to herself, as she
wandered about in the wood, вҖңis to grow to my right size again; and the
second thing is to find my way into that lovely garden. I think that
will be the best plan.вҖқ

It sounded an excellent plan, no doubt, and very neatly and simply
arranged; the only difficulty was, that she had not the smallest idea
how to set about it; and while she was peering about anxiously among
the trees, a little sharp bark just over her head made her look up in a
great hurry.

An enormous puppy was looking down at her with large round eyes, and
feebly stretching out one paw, trying to touch her. вҖңPoor little
thing!вҖқ said Alice, in a coaxing tone, and she tried hard to whistle to
it; but she was terribly frightened all the time at the thought that it
might be hungry, in which case it would be very likely to eat her up in
spite of all her coaxing.
4-13.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” White Rabbit ordering Alice about
4-13.prevIllustration: Harry Rountree ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Poor Lizard Bill
4-13.nextIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Bill the Lizard

4-14.chapterName: The Rabbit Sends in a Little Bill
4-14.text:
Hardly knowing what she did, she picked up a little bit of stick, and
held it out to the puppy; whereupon the puppy jumped into the air off
all its feet at once, with a yelp of delight, and rushed at the stick,
and made believe to worry it; then Alice dodged behind a great thistle,
to keep herself from being run over; and the moment she appeared on the
other side, the puppy made another rush at the stick, and tumbled head
over heels in its hurry to get hold of it; then Alice, thinking it was
very like having a game of play with a cart-horse, and expecting every
moment to be trampled under its feet, ran round the thistle again; then
the puppy began a series of short charges at the stick, running a very
little way forwards each time and a long way back, and barking hoarsely
all the while, till at last it sat down a good way off, panting, with
its tongue hanging out of its mouth, and its great eyes half shut.
4-14.illustrationDesc: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Bill the Lizard
4-14.prevIllustration: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” White Rabbit ordering Alice about

4-15.chapterName: The Rabbit Sends in a Little Bill
4-15.text:
This seemed to Alice a good opportunity for making her escape; so she
set off at once, and ran till she was quite tired and out of breath,
and till the puppyвҖҷs bark sounded quite faint in the distance. вҖңAnd yet what a dear little puppy it was!вҖқ said Alice, as she leant
against a buttercup to rest herself, and fanned herself with one of the
leaves: вҖңI should have liked teaching it tricks very much, ifвҖ”if IвҖҷd
only been the right size to do it! Oh dear! IвҖҷd nearly forgotten that
IвҖҷve got to grow up again! Let me seeвҖ”how is it to be managed? I
suppose I ought to eat or drink something or other; but the great
question is, what?вҖқ The great question certainly was, what? Alice looked all round her at
the flowers and the blades of grass, but she did not see anything that
looked like the right thing to eat or drink under the circumstances.
4-15.prevIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Bill the Lizard
4-15.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll (photograph) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice, Ina, Harry and Edith, 1860

4-16.chapterName: The Rabbit Sends in a Little Bill
4-16.text:
There was a large mushroom growing near her, about the same height as
herself; and when she had looked under it, and on both sides of it, and
behind it, it occurred to her that she might as well look and see what
was on the top of it. She stretched herself up on tiptoe, and peeped over the edge of the
mushroom, and her eyes immediately met those of a large blue
caterpillar, that was sitting on the top with its arms folded, quietly
smoking a long hookah, and taking not the smallest notice of her or of
anything else.
4-16.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll (photograph) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice, Ina, Harry and Edith, 1860

5-1.text:
The Caterpillar and Alice looked at each other for some time in
silence: at last the Caterpillar took the hookah out of its mouth, and
addressed her in a languid, sleepy voice.

вҖңWho are you?вҖқ said the Caterpillar.

This was not an encouraging opening for a conversation. Alice replied,
rather shyly, вҖңIвҖ”I hardly know, sir, just at presentвҖ”at least I know
who I was when I got up this morning, but I think I must have been
changed several times since then.вҖқ

вҖңWhat do you mean by that?вҖқ said the Caterpillar sternly. вҖңExplain
yourself!вҖқ

вҖңI canвҖҷt explain myself, IвҖҷm afraid, sir,вҖқ said Alice, вҖңbecause IвҖҷm
not myself, you see.вҖқ

вҖңI donвҖҷt see,вҖқ said the Caterpillar.

вҖңIвҖҷm afraid I canвҖҷt put it more clearly,вҖқ Alice replied very politely,
вҖңfor I canвҖҷt understand it myself to begin with; and being so many
different sizes in a day is very confusing.вҖқ

5-2.text:
вҖңIt isnвҖҷt,вҖқ said the Caterpillar.

вҖңWell, perhaps you havenвҖҷt found it so yet,вҖқ said Alice; вҖңbut when you
have to turn into a chrysalisвҖ”you will some day, you knowвҖ”and then
after that into a butterfly, I should think youвҖҷll feel it a little
queer, wonвҖҷt you?вҖқ

вҖңNot a bit,вҖқ said the Caterpillar.

вҖңWell, perhaps your feelings may be different,вҖқ said Alice; вҖңall I know
is, it would feel very queer to me.вҖқ

вҖңYou!вҖқ said the Caterpillar contemptuously. вҖңWho are you?вҖқ

Which brought them back again to the beginning of the conversation. Alice felt a little irritated at the CaterpillarвҖҷs making such very
short remarks, and she drew herself up and said, very gravely, вҖңI
think, you ought to tell me who you are, first.вҖқ

вҖңWhy?вҖқ said the Caterpillar.
5-2.nextIllustration: Gwynedd Hudson ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Advice from a caterpillar

5-3.text:
Here was another puzzling question; and as Alice could not think of any
good reason, and as the Caterpillar seemed to be in a very unpleasant
state of mind, she turned away.

вҖңCome back!вҖқ the Caterpillar called after her. вҖңIвҖҷve something
important to say!вҖқ

This sounded promising, certainly: Alice turned and came back again.

вҖңKeep your temper,вҖқ said the Caterpillar.

вҖңIs that all?вҖқ said Alice, swallowing down her anger as well as she
could.

вҖңNo,вҖқ said the Caterpillar.

Alice thought she might as well wait, as she had nothing else to do,
and perhaps after all it might tell her something worth hearing. For
some minutes it puffed away without speaking, but at last it unfolded
its arms, took the hookah out of its mouth again, and said, вҖңSo you
think youвҖҷre changed, do you?вҖқ
5-3.illustrationDesc: Gwynedd Hudson ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Advice from a caterpillar
5-3.nextIllustration: Arthur Rackham ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Advice from a Caterpillar

5-4.text:
вҖңIвҖҷm afraid I am, sir,вҖқ said Alice; вҖңI canвҖҷt remember things as I
usedвҖ”and I donвҖҷt keep the same size for ten minutes together!вҖқ

вҖңCanвҖҷt remember what things?вҖқ said the Caterpillar.

вҖңWell, IвҖҷve tried to say вҖңHow doth the little busy bee,вҖқ but it all
came different!вҖқ Alice replied in a very melancholy voice.

вҖңRepeat, вҖҳYou are old, Father William,вҖҷвҖқ said the Caterpillar.

Alice folded her hands, and began:вҖ”

вҖңYou are old, Father William,вҖқ the young man said,
    вҖңAnd your hair has become very white;
And yet you incessantly stand on your headвҖ”
    Do you think, at your age, it is right?вҖқ

вҖңIn my youth,вҖқ Father William replied to his son,
    вҖңI feared it might injure the brain;
But, now that IвҖҷm perfectly sure I have none,
    Why, I do it again and again.вҖқ
5-4.illustrationDesc: Arthur Rackham ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Advice from a Caterpillar
5-4.prevIllustration: Gwynedd Hudson ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Advice from a caterpillar

5-5.text:
вҖңYou are old,вҖқ said the youth, вҖңas I mentioned before,
    And have grown most uncommonly fat;
Yet you turned a back-somersault in at the doorвҖ”
    Pray, what is the reason of that?вҖқ

вҖңIn my youth,вҖқ said the sage, as he shook his grey locks,
    вҖңI kept all my limbs very supple
By the use of this ointmentвҖ”one shilling the boxвҖ”
    Allow me to sell you a couple?вҖқ

вҖңYou are old,вҖқ said the youth, вҖңand your jaws are too weak
    For anything tougher than suet;
Yet you finished the goose, with the bones and the beakвҖ”
    Pray, how did you manage to do it?вҖқ

вҖңIn my youth,вҖқ said his father, вҖңI took to the law,
    And argued each case with my wife;
And the muscular strength, which it gave to my jaw,
    Has lasted the rest of my life.вҖқ
5-5.prevIllustration: Arthur Rackham ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Advice from a Caterpillar
5-5.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Chapter II title page ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Advice from a Caterpillar

5-6.text:
вҖңYou are old,вҖқ said the youth, вҖңone would hardly suppose
    That your eye was as steady as ever;
Yet you balanced an eel on the end of your noseвҖ”
    What made you so awfully clever?вҖқ

вҖңI have answered three questions, and that is enough,вҖқ
    Said his father; вҖңdonвҖҷt give yourself airs!
Do you think I can listen all day to such stuff?
    Be off, or IвҖҷll kick you down stairs!вҖқ

вҖңThat is not said right,вҖқ said the Caterpillar.

вҖңNot quite right, IвҖҷm afraid,вҖқ said Alice, timidly; вҖңsome of the
words have got altered.вҖқ

вҖңIt is wrong from beginning to end,вҖқ said the Caterpillar decidedly,
and there was silence for some minutes.

The Caterpillar was the first to speak.

вҖңWhat size do you want to be?вҖқ it asked.

вҖңOh, IвҖҷm not particular as to size,вҖқ Alice hastily replied; вҖңonly one
doesnвҖҷt like changing so often, you know.вҖқ

вҖңI donвҖҷt know,вҖқ said the Caterpillar.
5-6.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Chapter II title page ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Advice from a Caterpillar
5-6.nextIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Caterpillar

5-7.text:
Alice said nothing: she had never been so much contradicted in her life
before, and she felt that she was losing her temper.

вҖңAre you content now?вҖқ said the Caterpillar.

вҖңWell, I should like to be a little larger, sir, if you wouldnвҖҷt
mind,вҖқ said Alice: вҖңthree inches is such a wretched height to be.вҖқ

вҖңIt is a very good height indeed!вҖқ said the Caterpillar angrily,
rearing itself upright as it spoke (it was exactly three inches high).

вҖңBut IвҖҷm not used to it!вҖқ pleaded poor Alice in a piteous tone. And she
thought of herself, вҖңI wish the creatures wouldnвҖҷt be so easily
offended!вҖқ

вҖңYouвҖҷll get used to it in time,вҖқ said the Caterpillar; and it put the
hookah into its mouth and began smoking again.

This time Alice waited patiently until it chose to speak again. In a
minute or two the Caterpillar took the hookah out of its mouth and
yawned once or twice, and shook itself. Then it got down off the
mushroom, and crawled away in the grass, merely remarking as it went,
вҖңOne side will make you grow taller, and the other side will make you
grow shorter.вҖқ
5-7.illustrationDesc: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Caterpillar
5-7.prevIllustration: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Chapter II title page ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Advice from a Caterpillar
5-7.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Pigeon calling Alice a serpent in the tree

5-8.text:
вҖңOne side of what? The other side of what?вҖқ thought Alice to
herself.

вҖңOf the mushroom,вҖқ said the Caterpillar, just as if she had asked it
aloud; and in another moment it was out of sight.

Alice remained looking thoughtfully at the mushroom for a minute,
trying to make out which were the two sides of it; and as it was
perfectly round, she found this a very difficult question. However, at
last she stretched her arms round it as far as they would go, and broke
off a bit of the edge with each hand.

вҖңAnd now which is which?вҖқ she said to herself, and nibbled a little of
the right-hand bit to try the effect: the next moment she felt a
violent blow underneath her chin: it had struck her foot!
5-8.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Pigeon calling Alice a serpent in the tree
5-8.prevIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Caterpillar
5-8.nextIllustration: GPT-4 (watercolor) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Pigeon accusing Alice of being a serpent

5-9.text:
She was a good deal frightened by this very sudden change, but she felt
that there was no time to be lost, as she was shrinking rapidly; so she
set to work at once to eat some of the other bit. Her chin was pressed
so closely against her foot, that there was hardly room to open her
mouth; but she did it at last, and managed to swallow a morsel of the
lefthand bit.

вҖңCome, my headвҖҷs free at last!вҖқ said Alice in a tone of delight, which
changed into alarm in another moment, when she found that her shoulders
were nowhere to be found: all she could see, when she looked down, was
an immense length of neck, which seemed to rise like a stalk out of a
sea of green leaves that lay far below her.

вҖңWhat can all that green stuff be?вҖқ said Alice. вҖңAnd where have my
shoulders got to? And oh, my poor hands, how is it I canвҖҷt see you?вҖқ She was moving them about as she spoke, but no result seemed to follow,
except a little shaking among the distant green leaves.
5-9.illustrationDesc: GPT-4 (watercolor) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Pigeon accusing Alice of being a serpent
5-9.prevIllustration: Lewis Carroll manuscript ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Pigeon calling Alice a serpent in the tree
5-9.nextIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГўВҖВ” She grew and grew

5-10.text:
As there seemed to be no chance of getting her hands up to her head,
she tried to get her head down to them, and was delighted to find that
her neck would bend about easily in any direction, like a serpent. She
had just succeeded in curving it down into a graceful zigzag, and was
going to dive in among the leaves, which she found to be nothing but
the tops of the trees under which she had been wandering, when a sharp
hiss made her draw back in a hurry: a large pigeon had flown into her
face, and was beating her violently with its wings.

вҖңSerpent!вҖқ screamed the Pigeon.

вҖңIвҖҷm not a serpent!вҖқ said Alice indignantly. вҖңLet me alone!вҖқ

вҖңSerpent, I say again!вҖқ repeated the Pigeon, but in a more subdued
tone, and added with a kind of sob, вҖңIвҖҷve tried every way, and nothing
seems to suit them!вҖқ

вҖңI havenвҖҷt the least idea what youвҖҷre talking about,вҖқ said Alice.
5-10.illustrationDesc: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГўВҖВ” She grew and grew
5-10.prevIllustration: GPT-4 (watercolor) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Pigeon accusing Alice of being a serpent

5-11.text:
вҖңIвҖҷve tried the roots of trees, and IвҖҷve tried banks, and IвҖҷve tried
hedges,вҖқ the Pigeon went on, without attending to her; вҖңbut those
serpents! ThereвҖҷs no pleasing them!вҖқ

Alice was more and more puzzled, but she thought there was no use in
saying anything more till the Pigeon had finished.

вҖңAs if it wasnвҖҷt trouble enough hatching the eggs,вҖқ said the Pigeon;
вҖңbut I must be on the look-out for serpents night and day! Why, I
havenвҖҷt had a wink of sleep these three weeks!вҖқ

вҖңIвҖҷm very sorry youвҖҷve been annoyed,вҖқ said Alice, who was beginning to
see its meaning.

вҖңAnd just as IвҖҷd taken the highest tree in the wood,вҖқ continued the
Pigeon, raising its voice to a shriek, вҖңand just as I was thinking I
should be free of them at last, they must needs come wriggling down
from the sky! Ugh, Serpent!вҖқ

вҖңBut IвҖҷm not a serpent, I tell you!вҖқ said Alice. вҖңIвҖҷm aвҖ”IвҖҷm aвҖ”вҖқ

вҖңWell! What are you?вҖқ said the Pigeon. вҖңI can see youвҖҷre trying to
invent something!вҖқ
5-11.prevIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГўВҖВ” She grew and grew

5-12.text:
вҖңIвҖ”IвҖҷm a little girl,вҖқ said Alice, rather doubtfully, as she remembered
the number of changes she had gone through that day. вҖңA likely story indeed!вҖқ said the Pigeon in a tone of the deepest
contempt. вҖңIвҖҷve seen a good many little girls in my time, but never
one with such a neck as that! No, no! YouвҖҷre a serpent; and thereвҖҷs
no use denying it. I suppose youвҖҷll be telling me next that you never
tasted an egg!вҖқ вҖңI have tasted eggs, certainly,вҖқ said Alice, who was a very truthful
child; вҖңbut little girls eat eggs quite as much as serpents do, you
know.вҖқ вҖңI donвҖҷt believe it,вҖқ said the Pigeon; вҖңbut if they do, why then
theyвҖҷre a kind of serpent, thatвҖҷs all I can say.вҖқ This was such a new idea to Alice, that she was quite silent for a
minute or two, which gave the Pigeon the opportunity of adding, вҖңYouвҖҷre
looking for eggs, I know that well enough; and what does it matter to
me whether youвҖҷre a little girl or a serpent?вҖқ

5-13.text:
вҖңIt matters a good deal to me,вҖқ said Alice hastily; вҖңbut IвҖҷm not
looking for eggs, as it happens; and if I was, I shouldnвҖҷt want
yours: I donвҖҷt like them raw.вҖқ вҖңWell, be off, then!вҖқ said the Pigeon in a sulky tone, as it settled
down again into its nest. Alice crouched down among the trees as well
as she could, for her neck kept getting entangled among the branches,
and every now and then she had to stop and untwist it. After a while
she remembered that she still held the pieces of mushroom in her hands,
and she set to work very carefully, nibbling first at one and then at
the other, and growing sometimes taller and sometimes shorter, until
she had succeeded in bringing herself down to her usual height. It was so long since she had been anything near the right size, that it
felt quite strange at first; but she got used to it in a few minutes,
and began talking to herself, as usual.

5-14.text:
вҖңCome, thereвҖҷs half my plan
done now! How puzzling all these changes are! IвҖҷm never sure what IвҖҷm
going to be, from one minute to another! However, IвҖҷve got back to my
right size: the next thing is, to get into that beautiful gardenвҖ”how
is that to be done, I wonder?вҖқ As she said this, she came suddenly
upon an open place, with a little house in it about four feet high. вҖңWhoever lives there,вҖқ thought Alice, вҖңitвҖҷll never do to come upon them
this size: why, I should frighten them out of their wits!вҖқ So she
began nibbling at the righthand bit again, and did not venture to go
near the house till she had brought herself down to nine inches high.

6-1.text:
For a minute or two she stood looking at the house, and wondering what
to do next, when suddenly a footman in livery came running out of the
woodвҖ”(she considered him to be a footman because he was in livery:
otherwise, judging by his face only, she would have called him a
fish)вҖ”and rapped loudly at the door with his knuckles. It was opened by
another footman in livery, with a round face, and large eyes like a
frog; and both footmen, Alice noticed, had powdered hair that curled
all over their heads. She felt very curious to know what it was all
about, and crept a little way out of the wood to listen.

The Fish-Footman began by producing from under his arm a great letter,
nearly as large as himself, and this he handed over to the other,
saying, in a solemn tone, вҖңFor the Duchess. An invitation from the
Queen to play croquet.вҖқ The Frog-Footman repeated, in the same solemn
tone, only changing the order of the words a little, вҖңFrom the Queen.
An invitation for the Duchess to play croquet.вҖқ
6-1.illustrationDesc: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Duchess & the Pig-baby
6-1.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Fish-Footman and Frog-Footman at the door

6-2.text:
Then they both bowed low, and their curls got entangled together.

Alice laughed so much at this, that she had to run back into the wood
for fear of their hearing her; and when she next peeped out the
Fish-Footman was gone, and the other was sitting on the ground near the
door, staring stupidly up into the sky.

Alice went timidly up to the door, and knocked.

вҖңThereвҖҷs no sort of use in knocking,вҖқ said the Footman, вҖңand that for
two reasons. First, because IвҖҷm on the same side of the door as you
are; secondly, because theyвҖҷre making such a noise inside, no one could
possibly hear you.вҖқ And certainly there was a most extraordinary
noise going on withinвҖ”a constant howling and sneezing, and every now
and then a great crash, as if a dish or kettle had been broken to
pieces.

вҖңPlease, then,вҖқ said Alice, вҖңhow am I to get in?вҖқ
6-2.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Fish-Footman and Frog-Footman at the door
6-2.prevIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Duchess & the Pig-baby
6-2.nextIllustration: Alice B. Woodward ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” You may nurse the baby, said Duchess

6-3.text:
вҖңThere might be some sense in your knocking,вҖқ the Footman went on
without attending to her, вҖңif we had the door between us. For instance,
if you were inside, you might knock, and I could let you out, you
know.вҖқ He was looking up into the sky all the time he was speaking, and
this Alice thought decidedly uncivil. вҖңBut perhaps he canвҖҷt help it,вҖқ
she said to herself; вҖңhis eyes are so very nearly at the top of his
head. But at any rate he might answer questions.вҖ”How am I to get in?вҖқ
she repeated, aloud.

вҖңI shall sit here,вҖқ the Footman remarked, вҖңtill tomorrowвҖ”вҖқ

At this moment the door of the house opened, and a large plate came
skimming out, straight at the FootmanвҖҷs head: it just grazed his nose,
and broke to pieces against one of the trees behind him.

вҖңвҖ”or next day, maybe,вҖқ the Footman continued in the same tone, exactly
as if nothing had happened.
6-3.illustrationDesc: Alice B. Woodward ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” You may nurse the baby, said Duchess
6-3.prevIllustration: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Fish-Footman and Frog-Footman at the door

6-4.text:
вҖңHow am I to get in?вҖқ asked Alice again, in a louder tone.

вҖңAre you to get in at all?вҖқ said the Footman. вҖңThatвҖҷs the first
question, you know.вҖқ

It was, no doubt: only Alice did not like to be told so. вҖңItвҖҷs really
dreadful,вҖқ she muttered to herself, вҖңthe way all the creatures argue.
ItвҖҷs enough to drive one crazy!вҖқ

The Footman seemed to think this a good opportunity for repeating his
remark, with variations. вҖңI shall sit here,вҖқ he said, вҖңon and off, for
days and days.вҖқ

вҖңBut what am I to do?вҖқ said Alice.

вҖңAnything you like,вҖқ said the Footman, and began whistling.

вҖңOh, thereвҖҷs no use in talking to him,вҖқ said Alice desperately: вҖңheвҖҷs
perfectly idiotic!вҖқ And she opened the door and went in.
6-4.prevIllustration: Alice B. Woodward ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” You may nurse the baby, said Duchess
6-4.nextIllustration: Arthur Rackham ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Saucepan flew by

6-5.text:
The door led right into a large kitchen, which was full of smoke from
one end to the other: the Duchess was sitting on a three-legged stool
in the middle, nursing a baby; the cook was leaning over the fire,
