stirring a large cauldron which seemed to be full of soup.

вҖңThereвҖҷs certainly too much pepper in that soup!вҖқ Alice said to
herself, as well as she could for sneezing.

There was certainly too much of it in the air. Even the Duchess sneezed
occasionally; and as for the baby, it was sneezing and howling
alternately without a momentвҖҷs pause. The only things in the kitchen
that did not sneeze, were the cook, and a large cat which was sitting
on the hearth and grinning from ear to ear.

вҖңPlease would you tell me,вҖқ said Alice, a little timidly, for she was
not quite sure whether it was good manners for her to speak first, вҖңwhy
your cat grins like that?вҖқ
6-5.illustrationDesc: Arthur Rackham ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Saucepan flew by
6-5.nextIllustration: Alice B. Woodward ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Shan't said the Cook

6-6.text:
вҖңItвҖҷs a Cheshire cat,вҖқ said the Duchess, вҖңand thatвҖҷs why. Pig!вҖқ

She said the last word with such sudden violence that Alice quite
jumped; but she saw in another moment that it was addressed to the
baby, and not to her, so she took courage, and went on again:вҖ”

вҖңI didnвҖҷt know that Cheshire cats always grinned; in fact, I didnвҖҷt
know that cats could grin.вҖқ

вҖңThey all can,вҖқ said the Duchess; вҖңand most of вҖҷem do.вҖқ

вҖңI donвҖҷt know of any that do,вҖқ Alice said very politely, feeling quite
pleased to have got into a conversation.

вҖңYou donвҖҷt know much,вҖқ said the Duchess; вҖңand thatвҖҷs a fact.вҖқ

Alice did not at all like the tone of this remark, and thought it would
be as well to introduce some other subject of conversation. While she
was trying to fix on one, the cook took the cauldron of soup off the
fire, and at once set to work throwing everything within her reach at
the Duchess and the babyвҖ”the fire-irons came first; then followed a
shower of saucepans, plates, and dishes.
6-6.illustrationDesc: Alice B. Woodward ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Shan't said the Cook
6-6.prevIllustration: Arthur Rackham ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Saucepan flew by
6-6.nextIllustration: GPT-4 (watercolor) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Cheshire Cat materializing in the Duchess's kitchen

6-7.text:
The Duchess took no notice of
them even when they hit her; and the baby was howling so much already,
that it was quite impossible to say whether the blows hurt it or not.

вҖңOh, please mind what youвҖҷre doing!вҖқ cried Alice, jumping up and down
in an agony of terror. вҖңOh, there goes his precious nose!вҖқ as an
unusually large saucepan flew close by it, and very nearly carried it
off.

вҖңIf everybody minded their own business,вҖқ the Duchess said in a hoarse
growl, вҖңthe world would go round a deal faster than it does.вҖқ

вҖңWhich would not be an advantage,вҖқ said Alice, who felt very glad to
get an opportunity of showing off a little of her knowledge. вҖңJust
think of what work it would make with the day and night! You see the
earth takes twenty-four hours to turn round on its axisвҖ”вҖқ

вҖңTalking of axes,вҖқ said the Duchess, вҖңchop off her head!вҖқ
6-7.illustrationDesc: GPT-4 (watercolor) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Cheshire Cat materializing in the Duchess's kitchen
6-7.prevIllustration: Alice B. Woodward ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Shan't said the Cook
6-7.nextIllustration: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Duchess's kitchen

6-8.text:
Alice glanced rather anxiously at the cook, to see if she meant to take
the hint; but the cook was busily stirring the soup, and seemed not to
be listening, so she went on again: вҖңTwenty-four hours, I think; or
is it twelve? IвҖ”вҖқ

вҖңOh, donвҖҷt bother me,вҖқ said the Duchess; вҖңI never could abide
figures!вҖқ And with that she began nursing her child again, singing a
sort of lullaby to it as she did so, and giving it a violent shake at
the end of every line:

вҖңSpeak roughly to your little boy,
    And beat him when he sneezes:
He only does it to annoy,
    Because he knows it teases.вҖқ

CHORUS. (In which the cook and the baby joined):

вҖңWow! wow! wow!вҖқ
6-8.illustrationDesc: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Duchess's kitchen
6-8.prevIllustration: GPT-4 (watercolor) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Cheshire Cat materializing in the Duchess's kitchen

6-9.text:
While the Duchess sang the second verse of the song, she kept tossing
the baby violently up and down, and the poor little thing howled so,
that Alice could hardly hear the words:вҖ”

вҖңI speak severely to my boy,
    I beat him when he sneezes;
For he can thoroughly enjoy
    The pepper when he pleases!вҖқ

CHORUS.

вҖңWow! wow! wow!вҖқ

вҖңHere! you may nurse it a bit, if you like!вҖқ the Duchess said to Alice,
flinging the baby at her as she spoke. вҖңI must go and get ready to play
croquet with the Queen,вҖқ and she hurried out of the room. The cook
threw a frying-pan after her as she went out, but it just missed her.

Alice caught the baby with some difficulty, as it was a queer-shaped
little creature, and held out its arms and legs in all directions,
вҖңjust like a star-fish,вҖқ thought Alice. The poor little thing was
snorting like a steam-engine when she caught it, and kept doubling
itself up and straightening itself out again, so that altogether, for
the first minute or two, it was as much as she could do to hold it.
6-9.prevIllustration: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Duchess's kitchen
6-9.nextIllustration: Gwynedd Hudson ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Duchess & the Baby

6-10.text:
As soon as she had made out the proper way of nursing it, (which was to
twist it up into a sort of knot, and then keep tight hold of its right
ear and left foot, so as to prevent its undoing itself,) she carried it
out into the open air. вҖңIf I donвҖҷt take this child away with me,вҖқ
thought Alice, вҖңtheyвҖҷre sure to kill it in a day or two: wouldnвҖҷt it be
murder to leave it behind?вҖқ She said the last words out loud, and the
little thing grunted in reply (it had left off sneezing by this time). вҖңDonвҖҷt grunt,вҖқ said Alice; вҖңthatвҖҷs not at all a proper way of
expressing yourself.вҖқ

The baby grunted again, and Alice looked very anxiously into its face
to see what was the matter with it. There could be no doubt that it had
a very turn-up nose, much more like a snout than a real nose; also
its eyes were getting extremely small for a baby: altogether Alice did
not like the look of the thing at all. вҖңBut perhaps it was only
sobbing,вҖқ she thought, and looked into its eyes again, to see if there
were any tears.
6-10.illustrationDesc: Gwynedd Hudson ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Duchess & the Baby
6-10.nextIllustration: Alice B. Woodward ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” You may nurse the baby said Duchess

6-11.text:
No, there were no tears. вҖңIf youвҖҷre going to turn into a pig, my dear,вҖқ
said Alice, seriously, вҖңIвҖҷll have nothing more to do with you. Mind
now!вҖқ The poor little thing sobbed again (or grunted, it was impossible
to say which), and they went on for some while in silence.

Alice was just beginning to think to herself, вҖңNow, what am I to do
with this creature when I get it home?вҖқ when it grunted again, so
violently, that she looked down into its face in some alarm. This time
there could be no mistake about it: it was neither more nor less than
a pig, and she felt that it would be quite absurd for her to carry it
further.

So she set the little creature down, and felt quite relieved to see it
trot away quietly into the wood. вҖңIf it had grown up,вҖқ she said to
herself, вҖңit would have made a dreadfully ugly child: but it makes
rather a handsome pig, I think.вҖқ
6-11.illustrationDesc: Alice B. Woodward ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” You may nurse the baby said Duchess
6-11.prevIllustration: Gwynedd Hudson ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Duchess & the Baby
6-11.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll (photograph) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice as a beggar-girl, 1858

6-12.text:
And she began thinking over other
children she knew, who might do very well as pigs, and was just saying
to herself, вҖңif one only knew the right way to change themвҖ”вҖқ when she
was a little startled by seeing the Cheshire Cat sitting on a bough of
a tree a few yards off.

The Cat only grinned when it saw Alice. It looked good-natured, she
thought: still it had very long claws and a great many teeth, so she
felt that it ought to be treated with respect.

вҖңCheshire Puss,вҖқ she began, rather timidly, as she did not at all know
whether it would like the name: however, it only grinned a little
wider. вҖңCome, itвҖҷs pleased so far,вҖқ thought Alice, and she went on. вҖңWould you tell me, please, which way I ought to go from here?вҖқ

вҖңThat depends a good deal on where you want to get to,вҖқ said the Cat.

вҖңI donвҖҷt much care whereвҖ”вҖқ said Alice.
6-12.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll (photograph) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice as a beggar-girl, 1858
6-12.prevIllustration: Alice B. Woodward ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” You may nurse the baby said Duchess
6-12.nextIllustration: John Tenniel & Harry Theaker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Cheshire Cat

6-13.text:
вҖңThen it doesnвҖҷt matter which way you go,вҖқ said the Cat.

вҖңвҖ”so long as I get somewhere,вҖқ Alice added as an explanation.

вҖңOh, youвҖҷre sure to do that,вҖқ said the Cat, вҖңif you only walk long
enough.вҖқ

Alice felt that this could not be denied, so she tried another
question. вҖңWhat sort of people live about here?вҖқ

вҖңIn that direction,вҖқ the Cat said, waving its right paw round, вҖңlives
a Hatter: and in that direction,вҖқ waving the other paw, вҖңlives a
March Hare. Visit either you like: theyвҖҷre both mad.вҖқ

вҖңBut I donвҖҷt want to go among mad people,вҖқ Alice remarked.

вҖңOh, you canвҖҷt help that,вҖқ said the Cat: вҖңweвҖҷre all mad here. IвҖҷm mad.
YouвҖҷre mad.вҖқ

вҖңHow do you know IвҖҷm mad?вҖқ said Alice.

вҖңYou must be,вҖқ said the Cat, вҖңor you wouldnвҖҷt have come here.вҖқ

Alice didnвҖҷt think that proved it at all; however, she went on вҖңAnd how
do you know that youвҖҷre mad?вҖқ
6-13.illustrationDesc: John Tenniel & Harry Theaker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Cheshire Cat
6-13.prevIllustration: Lewis Carroll (photograph) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice as a beggar-girl, 1858
6-13.nextIllustration: GPT-4 (watercolor) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice meets the Cheshire Cat in the tree

6-14.text:
вҖңTo begin with,вҖқ said the Cat, вҖңa dogвҖҷs not mad. You grant that?вҖқ вҖңI suppose so,вҖқ said Alice. вҖңWell, then,вҖқ the Cat went on, вҖңyou see, a dog growls when itвҖҷs angry,
and wags its tail when itвҖҷs pleased. Now I growl when IвҖҷm pleased,
and wag my tail when IвҖҷm angry. Therefore IвҖҷm mad.вҖқ вҖңI call it purring, not growling,вҖқ said Alice. вҖңCall it what you like,вҖқ said the Cat. вҖңDo you play croquet with the
Queen to-day?вҖқ вҖңI should like it very much,вҖқ said Alice, вҖңbut I havenвҖҷt been invited
yet.вҖқ вҖңYouвҖҷll see me there,вҖқ said the Cat, and vanished. Alice was not much surprised at this, she was getting so used to queer
things happening. While she was looking at the place where it had been,
it suddenly appeared again. вҖңBy-the-bye, what became of the baby?вҖқ said the Cat. вҖңIвҖҷd nearly
forgotten to ask.вҖқ вҖңIt turned into a pig,вҖқ Alice quietly said, just as if it had come back
in a natural way. вҖңI thought it would,вҖқ said the Cat, and vanished again.
6-14.illustrationDesc: GPT-4 (watercolor) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice meets the Cheshire Cat in the tree
6-14.prevIllustration: John Tenniel & Harry Theaker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Cheshire Cat

6-15.text:
Alice waited a little, half expecting to see it again, but it did not
appear, and after a minute or two she walked on in the direction in
which the March Hare was said to live. вҖңIвҖҷve seen hatters before,вҖқ she
said to herself; вҖңthe March Hare will be much the most interesting, and
perhaps as this is May it wonвҖҷt be raving madвҖ”at least not so mad as it
was in March.вҖқ As she said this, she looked up, and there was the Cat
again, sitting on a branch of a tree. вҖңDid you say pig, or fig?вҖқ said the Cat. вҖңI said pig,вҖқ replied Alice; вҖңand I wish you wouldnвҖҷt keep appearing
and vanishing so suddenly: you make one quite giddy.вҖқ вҖңAll right,вҖқ said the Cat; and this time it vanished quite slowly,
beginning with the end of the tail, and ending with the grin, which
remained some time after the rest of it had gone. вҖңWell! IвҖҷve often seen a cat without a grin,вҖқ thought Alice; вҖңbut a
grin without a cat! ItвҖҷs the most curious thing I ever saw in my life!вҖқ
6-15.prevIllustration: GPT-4 (watercolor) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice meets the Cheshire Cat in the tree
6-15.nextIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Cheshire Cat in tree

6-16.text:
She had not gone much farther before she came in sight of the house of
the March Hare: she thought it must be the right house, because the
chimneys were shaped like ears and the roof was thatched with fur. It
was so large a house, that she did not like to go nearer till she had
nibbled some more of the lefthand bit of mushroom, and raised herself
to about two feet high: even then she walked up towards it rather
timidly, saying to herself вҖңSuppose it should be raving mad after all!
I almost wish IвҖҷd gone to see the Hatter instead!вҖқ
6-16.illustrationDesc: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Cheshire Cat in tree

7-1.text:
There was a table set out under a tree in front of the house, and the
March Hare and the Hatter were having tea at it: a Dormouse was sitting
between them, fast asleep, and the other two were using it as a
cushion, resting their elbows on it, and talking over its head. вҖңVery
uncomfortable for the Dormouse,вҖқ thought Alice; вҖңonly, as itвҖҷs asleep,
I suppose it doesnвҖҷt mind.вҖқ

The table was a large one, but the three were all crowded together at
one corner of it: вҖңNo room! No room!вҖқ they cried out when they saw
Alice coming. вҖңThereвҖҷs plenty of room!вҖқ said Alice indignantly, and
she sat down in a large arm-chair at one end of the table.

вҖңHave some wine,вҖқ the March Hare said in an encouraging tone.

Alice looked all round the table, but there was nothing on it but tea. вҖңI donвҖҷt see any wine,вҖқ she remarked.

вҖңThere isnвҖҷt any,вҖқ said the March Hare.

вҖңThen it wasnвҖҷt very civil of you to offer it,вҖқ said Alice angrily.
7-1.illustrationDesc: Arthur Rackham ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Mad Hatter's tea party
7-1.nextIllustration: Alice B. Woodward ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” No room they cried

7-2.text:
вҖңIt wasnвҖҷt very civil of you to sit down without being invited,вҖқ said
the March Hare.

вҖңI didnвҖҷt know it was your table,вҖқ said Alice; вҖңitвҖҷs laid for a great
many more than three.вҖқ

вҖңYour hair wants cutting,вҖқ said the Hatter. He had been looking at
Alice for some time with great curiosity, and this was his first
speech.

вҖңYou should learn not to make personal remarks,вҖқ Alice said with some
severity; вҖңitвҖҷs very rude.вҖқ

The Hatter opened his eyes very wide on hearing this; but all he said
was, вҖңWhy is a raven like a writing-desk?вҖқ

вҖңCome, we shall have some fun now!вҖқ thought Alice. вҖңIвҖҷm glad theyвҖҷve
begun asking riddles.вҖ”I believe I can guess that,вҖқ she added aloud.

вҖңDo you mean that you think you can find out the answer to it?вҖқ said
the March Hare.

вҖңExactly so,вҖқ said Alice.

вҖңThen you should say what you mean,вҖқ the March Hare went on.
7-2.illustrationDesc: Alice B. Woodward ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” No room they cried
7-2.prevIllustration: Arthur Rackham ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Mad Hatter's tea party
7-2.nextIllustration: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” March Hare's house

7-3.text:
вҖңI do,вҖқ Alice hastily replied; вҖңat leastвҖ”at least I mean what I
sayвҖ”thatвҖҷs the same thing, you know.вҖқ

вҖңNot the same thing a bit!вҖқ said the Hatter. вҖңYou might just as well
say that вҖҳI see what I eatвҖҷ is the same thing as вҖҳI eat what I seeвҖҷ!вҖқ

вҖңYou might just as well say,вҖқ added the March Hare, вҖңthat вҖҳI like what
I getвҖҷ is the same thing as вҖҳI get what I likeвҖҷ!вҖқ

вҖңYou might just as well say,вҖқ added the Dormouse, who seemed to be
talking in his sleep, вҖңthat вҖҳI breathe when I sleepвҖҷ is the same thing
as вҖҳI sleep when I breatheвҖҷ!вҖқ

вҖңIt is the same thing with you,вҖқ said the Hatter, and here the
conversation dropped, and the party sat silent for a minute, while
Alice thought over all she could remember about ravens and
writing-desks, which wasnвҖҷt much.

The Hatter was the first to break the silence. вҖңWhat day of the month
is it?вҖқ he said, turning to Alice: he had taken his watch out of his
pocket, and was looking at it uneasily, shaking it every now and then,
and holding it to his ear.
7-3.illustrationDesc: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” March Hare's house
7-3.prevIllustration: Alice B. Woodward ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” No room they cried
7-3.nextIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Mad Hatter's Tea Party

7-4.text:
Alice considered a little, and then said вҖңThe fourth.вҖқ

вҖңTwo days wrong!вҖқ sighed the Hatter. вҖңI told you butter wouldnвҖҷt suit
the works!вҖқ he added looking angrily at the March Hare.

вҖңIt was the best butter,вҖқ the March Hare meekly replied.

вҖңYes, but some crumbs must have got in as well,вҖқ the Hatter grumbled:
вҖңyou shouldnвҖҷt have put it in with the bread-knife.вҖқ

The March Hare took the watch and looked at it gloomily: then he dipped
it into his cup of tea, and looked at it again: but he could think of
nothing better to say than his first remark, вҖңIt was the best butter,
you know.вҖқ

Alice had been looking over his shoulder with some curiosity. вҖңWhat a
funny watch!вҖқ she remarked. вҖңIt tells the day of the month, and doesnвҖҷt
tell what oвҖҷclock it is!вҖқ

вҖңWhy should it?вҖқ muttered the Hatter. вҖңDoes your watch tell you what
year it is?вҖқ
7-4.illustrationDesc: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Mad Hatter's Tea Party
7-4.prevIllustration: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” March Hare's house
7-4.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Duchess's chaotic kitchen

7-5.text:
вҖңOf course not,вҖқ Alice replied very readily: вҖңbut thatвҖҷs because it
stays the same year for such a long time together.вҖқ

вҖңWhich is just the case with mine,вҖқ said the Hatter.

Alice felt dreadfully puzzled, The HatterвҖҷs remark seemed to have no
sort of meaning in it, and yet it was certainly English. вҖңI donвҖҷt quite
understand you,вҖқ she said, as politely as she could.

вҖңThe Dormouse is asleep again,вҖқ said the Hatter, and he poured a little
hot tea upon its nose.

The Dormouse shook its head impatiently, and said, without opening its
eyes, вҖңOf course, of course; just what I was going to remark myself.вҖқ

вҖңHave you guessed the riddle yet?вҖқ the Hatter said, turning to Alice
again.

вҖңNo, I give it up,вҖқ Alice replied: вҖңwhatвҖҷs the answer?вҖқ

вҖңI havenвҖҷt the slightest idea,вҖқ said the Hatter.

вҖңNor I,вҖқ said the March Hare.

Alice sighed wearily. вҖңI think you might do something better with the
time,вҖқ she said, вҖңthan waste it in asking riddles that have no
answers.вҖқ
7-5.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Duchess's chaotic kitchen
7-5.prevIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Mad Hatter's Tea Party
7-5.nextIllustration: William H. Walker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Hatter's Tea Party

7-6.text:
вҖңIf you knew Time as well as I do,вҖқ said the Hatter, вҖңyou wouldnвҖҷt talk
about wasting it. ItвҖҷs him.вҖқ

вҖңI donвҖҷt know what you mean,вҖқ said Alice.

вҖңOf course you donвҖҷt!вҖқ the Hatter said, tossing his head
contemptuously. вҖңI dare say you never even spoke to Time!вҖқ

вҖңPerhaps not,вҖқ Alice cautiously replied: вҖңbut I know I have to beat
time when I learn music.вҖқ

вҖңAh! that accounts for it,вҖқ said the Hatter. вҖңHe wonвҖҷt stand beating.
Now, if you only kept on good terms with him, heвҖҷd do almost anything
you liked with the clock. For instance, suppose it were nine oвҖҷclock in
the morning, just time to begin lessons: youвҖҷd only have to whisper a
hint to Time, and round goes the clock in a twinkling! Half-past one,
time for dinner!вҖқ

(вҖңI only wish it was,вҖқ the March Hare said to itself in a whisper.)

вҖңThat would be grand, certainly,вҖқ said Alice thoughtfully: вҖңbut thenвҖ”I
shouldnвҖҷt be hungry for it, you know.вҖқ
7-6.illustrationDesc: William H. Walker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Hatter's Tea Party
7-6.prevIllustration: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Duchess's chaotic kitchen
7-6.nextIllustration: John Tenniel ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Mad Hatter's Tea Party

7-7.text:
вҖңNot at first, perhaps,вҖқ said the Hatter: вҖңbut you could keep it to
half-past one as long as you liked.вҖқ

вҖңIs that the way you manage?вҖқ Alice asked.

The Hatter shook his head mournfully. вҖңNot I!вҖқ he replied. вҖңWe
quarrelled last MarchвҖ”just before he went mad, you knowвҖ”вҖқ (pointing
with his tea spoon at the March Hare,) вҖңвҖ”it was at the great concert
given by the Queen of Hearts, and I had to sing

вҖҳTwinkle, twinkle, little bat!
How I wonder what youвҖҷre at!вҖҷ

You know the song, perhaps?вҖқ

вҖңIвҖҷve heard something like it,вҖқ said Alice.

вҖңIt goes on, you know,вҖқ the Hatter continued, вҖңin this way:вҖ”

вҖҳUp above the world you fly,
Like a tea-tray in the sky.
                    Twinkle, twinkleвҖ”вҖҷвҖқ

Here the Dormouse shook itself, and began singing in its sleep
вҖңTwinkle, twinkle, twinkle, twinkleвҖ”вҖқ and went on so long that they
had to pinch it to make it stop.
7-7.illustrationDesc: John Tenniel ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Mad Hatter's Tea Party
7-7.prevIllustration: William H. Walker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Hatter's Tea Party
7-7.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Animals gathered for a concert

7-8.text:
вҖңWell, IвҖҷd hardly finished the first verse,вҖқ said the Hatter, вҖңwhen the
Queen jumped up and bawled out, вҖҳHeвҖҷs murdering the time! Off with his
head!вҖҷвҖқ

вҖңHow dreadfully savage!вҖқ exclaimed Alice.

вҖңAnd ever since that,вҖқ the Hatter went on in a mournful tone, вҖңhe wonвҖҷt
do a thing I ask! ItвҖҷs always six oвҖҷclock now.вҖқ

A bright idea came into AliceвҖҷs head. вҖңIs that the reason so many
tea-things are put out here?вҖқ she asked.

вҖңYes, thatвҖҷs it,вҖқ said the Hatter with a sigh: вҖңitвҖҷs always tea-time,
and weвҖҷve no time to wash the things between whiles.вҖқ

вҖңThen you keep moving round, I suppose?вҖқ said Alice.

вҖңExactly so,вҖқ said the Hatter: вҖңas the things get used up.вҖқ

вҖңBut what happens when you come to the beginning again?вҖқ Alice ventured
to ask.

вҖңSuppose we change the subject,вҖқ the March Hare interrupted, yawning. вҖңIвҖҷm getting tired of this. I vote the young lady tells us a story.вҖқ
7-8.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Animals gathered for a concert
7-8.prevIllustration: John Tenniel ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Mad Hatter's Tea Party

7-9.text:
вҖңIвҖҷm afraid I donвҖҷt know one,вҖқ said Alice, rather alarmed at the
proposal.

вҖңThen the Dormouse shall!вҖқ they both cried. вҖңWake up, Dormouse!вҖқ And
they pinched it on both sides at once.

The Dormouse slowly opened his eyes. вҖңI wasnвҖҷt asleep,вҖқ he said in a
hoarse, feeble voice: вҖңI heard every word you fellows were saying.вҖқ

вҖңTell us a story!вҖқ said the March Hare.

вҖңYes, please do!вҖқ pleaded Alice.

вҖңAnd be quick about it,вҖқ added the Hatter, вҖңor youвҖҷll be asleep again
before itвҖҷs done.вҖқ

вҖңOnce upon a time there were three little sisters,вҖқ the Dormouse began
in a great hurry; вҖңand their names were Elsie, Lacie, and Tillie; and
they lived at the bottom of a wellвҖ”вҖқ

вҖңWhat did they live on?вҖқ said Alice, who always took a great interest
in questions of eating and drinking.

вҖңThey lived on treacle,вҖқ said the Dormouse, after thinking a minute or
two.
7-9.prevIllustration: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Animals gathered for a concert
7-9.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice with sister by the river

7-10.text:
вҖңThey couldnвҖҷt have done that, you know,вҖқ Alice gently remarked;
вҖңtheyвҖҷd have been ill.вҖқ

вҖңSo they were,вҖқ said the Dormouse; вҖңvery ill.вҖқ

Alice tried to fancy to herself what such an extraordinary way of
living would be like, but it puzzled her too much, so she went on: вҖңBut
why did they live at the bottom of a well?вҖқ

вҖңTake some more tea,вҖқ the March Hare said to Alice, very earnestly.

вҖңIвҖҷve had nothing yet,вҖқ Alice replied in an offended tone, вҖңso I canвҖҷt
take more.вҖқ

вҖңYou mean you canвҖҷt take less,вҖқ said the Hatter: вҖңitвҖҷs very easy to
take more than nothing.вҖқ

вҖңNobody asked your opinion,вҖқ said Alice.

вҖңWhoвҖҷs making personal remarks now?вҖқ the Hatter asked triumphantly.

Alice did not quite know what to say to this: so she helped herself to
some tea and bread-and-butter, and then turned to the Dormouse, and
repeated her question. вҖңWhy did they live at the bottom of a well?вҖқ
7-10.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice with sister by the river
7-10.nextIllustration: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Dormouse put in teapot

7-11.text:
The Dormouse again took a minute or two to think about it, and then
said, вҖңIt was a treacle-well.вҖқ

вҖңThereвҖҷs no such thing!вҖқ Alice was beginning very angrily, but the
Hatter and the March Hare went вҖңSh! sh!вҖқ and the Dormouse sulkily
remarked, вҖңIf you canвҖҷt be civil, youвҖҷd better finish the story for
yourself.вҖқ

вҖңNo, please go on!вҖқ Alice said very humbly; вҖңI wonвҖҷt interrupt again. I
dare say there may be one.вҖқ

вҖңOne, indeed!вҖқ said the Dormouse indignantly. However, he consented to
go on. вҖңAnd so these three little sistersвҖ”they were learning to draw,
you knowвҖ”вҖқ

вҖңWhat did they draw?вҖқ said Alice, quite forgetting her promise.

вҖңTreacle,вҖқ said the Dormouse, without considering at all this time.

вҖңI want a clean cup,вҖқ interrupted the Hatter: вҖңletвҖҷs all move one place
on.вҖқ
7-11.illustrationDesc: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Dormouse put in teapot
7-11.prevIllustration: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice with sister by the river
7-11.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll (photograph) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Lorina, Edith and Alice, 1859

7-12.text:
He moved on as he spoke, and the Dormouse followed him: the March Hare
moved into the DormouseвҖҷs place, and Alice rather unwillingly took the
place of the March Hare. The Hatter was the only one who got any
advantage from the change: and Alice was a good deal worse off than
before, as the March Hare had just upset the milk-jug into his plate. Alice did not wish to offend the Dormouse again, so she began very
cautiously: вҖңBut I donвҖҷt understand. Where did they draw the treacle
from?вҖқ вҖңYou can draw water out of a water-well,вҖқ said the Hatter; вҖңso I should
think you could draw treacle out of a treacle-wellвҖ”eh, stupid?вҖқ вҖңBut they were in the well,вҖқ Alice said to the Dormouse, not choosing
to notice this last remark. вҖңOf course they were,вҖқ said the Dormouse; вҖңвҖ”well in.вҖқ This answer so confused poor Alice, that she let the Dormouse go on for
some time without interrupting it.
7-12.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll (photograph) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Lorina, Edith and Alice, 1859
7-12.prevIllustration: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Dormouse put in teapot
7-12.nextIllustration: Harry Rountree ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Dormouse is asleep

7-13.text:
вҖңThey were learning to draw,вҖқ the Dormouse went on, yawning and rubbing
its eyes, for it was getting very sleepy; вҖңand they drew all manner of
thingsвҖ”everything that begins with an MвҖ”вҖқ

вҖңWhy with an M?вҖқ said Alice. вҖңWhy not?вҖқ said the March Hare. Alice was silent. The Dormouse had closed its eyes by this time, and was going off into a
doze; but, on being pinched by the Hatter, it woke up again with a
little shriek, and went on: вҖңвҖ”that begins with an M, such as
mouse-traps, and the moon, and memory, and muchnessвҖ”you know you say
things are вҖңmuch of a muchnessвҖқвҖ”did you ever see such a thing as a
drawing of a muchness?вҖқ вҖңReally, now you ask me,вҖқ said Alice, very much confused, вҖңI donвҖҷt
thinkвҖ”вҖқ

вҖңThen you shouldnвҖҷt talk,вҖқ said the Hatter.
7-13.illustrationDesc: Harry Rountree ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Dormouse is asleep
7-13.prevIllustration: Lewis Carroll (photograph) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Lorina, Edith and Alice, 1859

7-14.text:
This piece of rudeness was more than Alice could bear: she got up in
great disgust, and walked off; the Dormouse fell asleep instantly, and
neither of the others took the least notice of her going, though she
looked back once or twice, half hoping that they would call after her:
the last time she saw them, they were trying to put the Dormouse into
the teapot. вҖңAt any rate IвҖҷll never go there again!вҖқ said Alice as she picked her
way through the wood. вҖңItвҖҷs the stupidest tea-party I ever was at in
all my life!вҖқ Just as she said this, she noticed that one of the trees had a door
leading right into it. вҖңThatвҖҷs very curious!вҖқ she thought. вҖңBut
everythingвҖҷs curious today. I think I may as well go in at once.вҖқ And
in she went. Once more she found herself in the long hall, and close to the little
glass table. вҖңNow, IвҖҷll manage better this time,вҖқ she said to herself,
and began by taking the little golden key, and unlocking the door that
led into the garden.

Then she went to work nibbling at the mushroom
(she had kept a piece of it in her pocket) till she was about a foot
high: then she walked down the little passage: and thenвҖ”she found
herself at last in the beautiful garden, among the bright flower-beds
and the cool fountains.
7-14.prevIllustration: Harry Rountree ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Dormouse is asleep

8-1.text:
A large rose-tree stood near the entrance of the garden: the roses
growing on it were white, but there were three gardeners at it, busily
painting them red. Alice thought this a very curious thing, and she
went nearer to watch them, and just as she came up to them she heard
one of them say, вҖңLook out now, Five! DonвҖҷt go splashing paint over me
like that!вҖқ

вҖңI couldnвҖҷt help it,вҖқ said Five, in a sulky tone; вҖңSeven jogged my
elbow.вҖқ

On which Seven looked up and said, вҖңThatвҖҷs right, Five! Always lay the
blame on others!вҖқ

вҖңYouвҖҷd better not talk!вҖқ said Five. вҖңI heard the Queen say only
yesterday you deserved to be beheaded!вҖқ

вҖңWhat for?вҖқ said the one who had spoken first.

вҖңThatвҖҷs none of your business, Two!вҖқ said Seven.

вҖңYes, it is his business!вҖқ said Five, вҖңand IвҖҷll tell himвҖ”it was for
bringing the cook tulip-roots instead of onions.вҖқ
8-1.illustrationDesc: Gwynedd Hudson ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Gardeners painting roses red
8-1.nextIllustration: William H. Walker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Playing cards

8-2.text:
Seven flung down his brush, and had just begun вҖңWell, of all the unjust
thingsвҖ”вҖқ when his eye chanced to fall upon Alice, as she stood watching
them, and he checked himself suddenly: the others looked round also,
and all of them bowed low.

вҖңWould you tell me,вҖқ said Alice, a little timidly, вҖңwhy you are
painting those roses?вҖқ

Five and Seven said nothing, but looked at Two. Two began in a low
voice, вҖңWhy the fact is, you see, Miss, this here ought to have been a
red rose-tree, and we put a white one in by mistake; and if the Queen
was to find it out, we should all have our heads cut off, you know. So
you see, Miss, weвҖҷre doing our best, afore she comes, toвҖ”вҖқ At this
moment Five, who had been anxiously looking across the garden, called
out вҖңThe Queen! The Queen!вҖқ and the three gardeners instantly threw
themselves flat upon their faces. There was a sound of many footsteps,
and Alice looked round, eager to see the Queen.
8-2.illustrationDesc: William H. Walker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Playing cards
8-2.prevIllustration: Gwynedd Hudson ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Gardeners painting roses red
8-2.nextIllustration: Arthur Rackham ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Queen of Hearts

8-3.text:
First came ten soldiers carrying clubs; these were all shaped like the
three gardeners, oblong and flat, with their hands and feet at the
corners: next the ten courtiers; these were ornamented all over with
diamonds, and walked two and two, as the soldiers did. After these came
the royal children; there were ten of them, and the little dears came
jumping merrily along hand in hand, in couples: they were all
ornamented with hearts. Next came the guests, mostly Kings and Queens,
and among them Alice recognised the White Rabbit: it was talking in a
hurried nervous manner, smiling at everything that was said, and went
by without noticing her. Then followed the Knave of Hearts, carrying
the KingвҖҷs crown on a crimson velvet cushion; and, last of all this
grand procession, came THE KING AND QUEEN OF HEARTS.
8-3.illustrationDesc: Arthur Rackham ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Queen of Hearts
8-3.prevIllustration: William H. Walker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Playing cards
8-3.nextIllustration: Bessie Pease Gutmann ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Shan't be beheaded

8-4.text:
Alice was rather doubtful whether she ought not to lie down on her face
like the three gardeners, but she could not remember ever having heard
of such a rule at processions; вҖңand besides, what would be the use of a
procession,вҖқ thought she, вҖңif people had all to lie down upon their
faces, so that they couldnвҖҷt see it?вҖқ So she stood still where she was,
and waited.

When the procession came opposite to Alice, they all stopped and looked
at her, and the Queen said severely вҖңWho is this?вҖқ She said it to the
Knave of Hearts, who only bowed and smiled in reply.

вҖңIdiot!вҖқ said the Queen, tossing her head impatiently; and, turning to
Alice, she went on, вҖңWhatвҖҷs your name, child?вҖқ

вҖңMy name is Alice, so please your Majesty,вҖқ said Alice very politely;
but she added, to herself, вҖңWhy, theyвҖҷre only a pack of cards, after
all. I neednвҖҷt be afraid of them!вҖқ
8-4.illustrationDesc: Bessie Pease Gutmann ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Shan't be beheaded
8-4.prevIllustration: Arthur Rackham ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Queen of Hearts
8-4.nextIllustration: Gwynedd Hudson ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” King & Queen of Hearts

8-5.text:
вҖңAnd who are these?вҖқ said the Queen, pointing to the three gardeners
who were lying round the rose-tree; for, you see, as they were lying on
their faces, and the pattern on their backs was the same as the rest of
the pack, she could not tell whether they were gardeners, or soldiers,
or courtiers, or three of her own children.

вҖңHow should I know?вҖқ said Alice, surprised at her own courage. вҖңItвҖҷs
no business of mine.вҖқ

The Queen turned crimson with fury, and, after glaring at her for a
moment like a wild beast, screamed вҖңOff with her head! OffвҖ”вҖқ

вҖңNonsense!вҖқ said Alice, very loudly and decidedly, and the Queen was
silent.

The King laid his hand upon her arm, and timidly said вҖңConsider, my
dear: she is only a child!вҖқ

The Queen turned angrily away from him, and said to the Knave вҖңTurn
them over!вҖқ

The Knave did so, very carefully, with one foot.
8-5.illustrationDesc: Gwynedd Hudson ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” King & Queen of Hearts
8-5.prevIllustration: Bessie Pease Gutmann ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Shan't be beheaded
8-5.nextIllustration: John Tenniel & Harry Theaker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Off with her head

8-6.text:
вҖңGet up!вҖқ said the Queen, in a shrill, loud voice, and the three
gardeners instantly jumped up, and began bowing to the King, the Queen,
the royal children, and everybody else.

вҖңLeave off that!вҖқ screamed the Queen. вҖңYou make me giddy.вҖқ And then,
turning to the rose-tree, she went on, вҖңWhat have you been doing
here?вҖқ

вҖңMay it please your Majesty,вҖқ said Two, in a very humble tone, going
down on one knee as he spoke, вҖңwe were tryingвҖ”вҖқ

вҖңI see!вҖқ said the Queen, who had meanwhile been examining the roses. вҖңOff with their heads!вҖқ and the procession moved on, three of the
soldiers remaining behind to execute the unfortunate gardeners, who ran
to Alice for protection.

вҖңYou shanвҖҷt be beheaded!вҖқ said Alice, and she put them into a large
flower-pot that stood near. The three soldiers wandered about for a
minute or two, looking for them, and then quietly marched off after the
others.

вҖңAre their heads off?вҖқ shouted the Queen.
8-6.illustrationDesc: John Tenniel & Harry Theaker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Off with her head
8-6.prevIllustration: Gwynedd Hudson ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” King & Queen of Hearts
8-6.nextIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Queen has come

8-7.text:
вҖңTheir heads are gone, if it please your Majesty!вҖқ the soldiers shouted
in reply.

вҖңThatвҖҷs right!вҖқ shouted the Queen. вҖңCan you play croquet?вҖқ

The soldiers were silent, and looked at Alice, as the question was
evidently meant for her.

вҖңYes!вҖқ shouted Alice.

вҖңCome on, then!вҖқ roared the Queen, and Alice joined the procession,
wondering very much what would happen next.

вҖңItвҖҷsвҖ”itвҖҷs a very fine day!вҖқ said a timid voice at her side. She was
walking by the White Rabbit, who was peeping anxiously into her face.

вҖңVery,вҖқ said Alice: вҖңвҖ”whereвҖҷs the Duchess?вҖқ

вҖңHush! Hush!вҖқ said the Rabbit in a low, hurried tone. He looked
anxiously over his shoulder as he spoke, and then raised himself upon
tiptoe, put his mouth close to her ear, and whispered вҖңSheвҖҷs under
sentence of execution.вҖқ

вҖңWhat for?вҖқ said Alice.

вҖңDid you say вҖҳWhat a pity!вҖҷ?вҖқ the Rabbit asked.
8-7.illustrationDesc: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Queen has come
8-7.prevIllustration: John Tenniel & Harry Theaker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Off with her head
8-7.nextIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Live cards with red rose-tree

8-8.text:
вҖңNo, I didnвҖҷt,вҖқ said Alice: вҖңI donвҖҷt think itвҖҷs at all a pity. I said
вҖҳWhat for?вҖҷвҖқ

вҖңShe boxed the QueenвҖҷs earsвҖ”вҖқ the Rabbit began. Alice gave a little
scream of laughter. вҖңOh, hush!вҖқ the Rabbit whispered in a frightened
tone. вҖңThe Queen will hear you! You see, she came rather late, and the
Queen saidвҖ”вҖқ

вҖңGet to your places!вҖқ shouted the Queen in a voice of thunder, and
people began running about in all directions, tumbling up against each
other; however, they got settled down in a minute or two, and the game
began. Alice thought she had never seen such a curious croquet-ground
in her life; it was all ridges and furrows; the balls were live
hedgehogs, the mallets live flamingoes, and the soldiers had to double
themselves up and to stand on their hands and feet, to make the arches.
8-8.illustrationDesc: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Live cards with red rose-tree
8-8.prevIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Queen has come
8-8.nextIllustration: John Tenniel & Harry Theaker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Invitation to play croquet

8-9.text:
The chief difficulty Alice found at first was in managing her flamingo:
she succeeded in getting its body tucked away, comfortably enough,
under her arm, with its legs hanging down, but generally, just as she
had got its neck nicely straightened out, and was going to give the
hedgehog a blow with its head, it would twist itself round and look
up in her face, with such a puzzled expression that she could not help
bursting out laughing: and when she had got its head down, and was
going to begin again, it was very provoking to find that the hedgehog
had unrolled itself, and was in the act of crawling away: besides all
this, there was generally a ridge or furrow in the way wherever she
wanted to send the hedgehog to, and, as the doubled-up soldiers were
always getting up and walking off to other parts of the ground, Alice
soon came to the conclusion that it was a very difficult game indeed.
8-9.illustrationDesc: John Tenniel & Harry Theaker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Invitation to play croquet
8-9.prevIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Live cards with red rose-tree
8-9.nextIllustration: John Tenniel & Harry Theaker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” King & Queen of Hearts

8-10.text:
The players all played at once without waiting for turns, quarrelling
all the while, and fighting for the hedgehogs; and in a very short time
the Queen was in a furious passion, and went stamping about, and
shouting вҖңOff with his head!вҖқ or вҖңOff with her head!вҖқ about once in a
minute.

Alice began to feel very uneasy: to be sure, she had not as yet had any
dispute with the Queen, but she knew that it might happen any minute,
вҖңand then,вҖқ thought she, вҖңwhat would become of me? TheyвҖҷre dreadfully
fond of beheading people here; the great wonder is, that thereвҖҷs any
one left alive!вҖқ
8-10.illustrationDesc: John Tenniel & Harry Theaker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” King & Queen of Hearts
8-10.prevIllustration: John Tenniel & Harry Theaker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Invitation to play croquet
8-10.nextIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice, the Duchess & the Flamingo

8-11.text:
She was looking about for some way of escape, and wondering whether she
could get away without being seen, when she noticed a curious
appearance in the air: it puzzled her very much at first, but, after
watching it a minute or two, she made it out to be a grin, and she said
to herself вҖңItвҖҷs the Cheshire Cat: now I shall have somebody to talk
to.вҖқ

вҖңHow are you getting on?вҖқ said the Cat, as soon as there was mouth
enough for it to speak with.

Alice waited till the eyes appeared, and then nodded. вҖңItвҖҷs no use
speaking to it,вҖқ she thought, вҖңtill its ears have come, or at least one
of them.вҖқ In another minute the whole head appeared, and then Alice put
down her flamingo, and began an account of the game, feeling very glad
she had someone to listen to her. The Cat seemed to think that there
was enough of it now in sight, and no more of it appeared.
8-11.illustrationDesc: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice, the Duchess & the Flamingo
8-11.prevIllustration: John Tenniel & Harry Theaker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” King & Queen of Hearts
8-11.nextIllustration: GPT-4 ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Queen of Hearts playing card with flamingo

8-12.text:
вҖңI donвҖҷt think they play at all fairly,вҖқ Alice began, in rather a
complaining tone, вҖңand they all quarrel so dreadfully one canвҖҷt hear
oneself speakвҖ”and they donвҖҷt seem to have any rules in particular; at
least, if there are, nobody attends to themвҖ”and youвҖҷve no idea how
confusing it is all the things being alive; for instance, thereвҖҷs the
arch IвҖҷve got to go through next walking about at the other end of the
groundвҖ”and I should have croqueted the QueenвҖҷs hedgehog just now, only
it ran away when it saw mine coming!вҖқ

вҖңHow do you like the Queen?вҖқ said the Cat in a low voice.

вҖңNot at all,вҖқ said Alice: вҖңsheвҖҷs so extremelyвҖ”вҖқ Just then she noticed
that the Queen was close behind her, listening: so she went on,
вҖңвҖ”likely to win, that itвҖҷs hardly worth while finishing the game.вҖқ

The Queen smiled and passed on.

вҖңWho are you talking to?вҖқ said the King, going up to Alice, and
looking at the CatвҖҷs head with great curiosity.
8-12.illustrationDesc: GPT-4 ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Queen of Hearts playing card with flamingo
8-12.prevIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice, the Duchess & the Flamingo
8-12.nextIllustration: Gwynedd Hudson ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” King Queen Executioner

8-13.text:
вҖңItвҖҷs a friend of mineвҖ”a Cheshire Cat,вҖқ said Alice: вҖңallow me to
introduce it.вҖқ вҖңI donвҖҷt like the look of it at all,вҖқ said the King: вҖңhowever, it may
kiss my hand if it likes.вҖқ вҖңIвҖҷd rather not,вҖқ the Cat remarked. вҖңDonвҖҷt be impertinent,вҖқ said the King, вҖңand donвҖҷt look at me like
that!вҖқ He got behind Alice as he spoke. вҖңA cat may look at a king,вҖқ said Alice. вҖңIвҖҷve read that in some book,
but I donвҖҷt remember where.вҖқ вҖңWell, it must be removed,вҖқ said the King very decidedly, and he called
the Queen, who was passing at the moment, вҖңMy dear! I wish you would
have this cat removed!вҖқ The Queen had only one way of settling all difficulties, great or
small. вҖңOff with his head!вҖқ she said, without even looking round. вҖңIвҖҷll fetch the executioner myself,вҖқ said the King eagerly, and he
hurried off. Alice thought she might as well go back, and see how the game was going
on, as she heard the QueenвҖҷs voice in the distance, screaming with
passion.
8-13.illustrationDesc: Gwynedd Hudson ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” King Queen Executioner
8-13.prevIllustration: GPT-4 ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Queen of Hearts playing card with flamingo
8-13.nextIllustration: GPT-4 (watercolor) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Cheshire Cat's grin above the croquet ground

8-14.text:
She had already heard her sentence three of the players to be
executed for having missed their turns, and she did not like the look
of things at all, as the game was in such confusion that she never knew
whether it was her turn or not. So she went in search of her hedgehog. The hedgehog was engaged in a fight with another hedgehog, which seemed
to Alice an excellent opportunity for croqueting one of them with the
other: the only difficulty was, that her flamingo was gone across to
the other side of the garden, where Alice could see it trying in a
helpless sort of way to fly up into a tree. By the time she had caught the flamingo and brought it back, the fight
was over, and both the hedgehogs were out of sight: вҖңbut it doesnвҖҷt
matter much,вҖқ thought Alice, вҖңas all the arches are gone from this side
of the ground.вҖқ So she tucked it away under her arm, that it might not
escape again, and went back for a little more conversation with her
friend.
8-14.illustrationDesc: GPT-4 (watercolor) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Cheshire Cat's grin above the croquet ground
8-14.prevIllustration: Gwynedd Hudson ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” King Queen Executioner
8-14.nextIllustration: Alice B. Woodward ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Executioner came back with Duchess

8-15.text:
When she got back to the Cheshire Cat, she was surprised to find quite
a large crowd collected round it: there was a dispute going on between
the executioner, the King, and the Queen, who were all talking at once,
while all the rest were quite silent, and looked very uncomfortable. The moment Alice appeared, she was appealed to by all three to settle
the question, and they repeated their arguments to her, though, as they
all spoke at once, she found it very hard indeed to make out exactly
what they said. The executionerвҖҷs argument was, that you couldnвҖҷt cut off a head unless
there was a body to cut it off from: that he had never had to do such a
thing before, and he wasnвҖҷt going to begin at his time of life. The KingвҖҷs argument was, that anything that had a head could be
beheaded, and that you werenвҖҷt to talk nonsense. The QueenвҖҷs argument was, that if something wasnвҖҷt done about it in
less than no time sheвҖҷd have everybody executed, all round.
8-15.illustrationDesc: Alice B. Woodward ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Executioner came back with Duchess
8-15.prevIllustration: GPT-4 (watercolor) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Cheshire Cat's grin above the croquet ground

8-16.text:
(It was
this last remark that had made the whole party look so grave and
anxious.) Alice could think of nothing else to say but вҖңIt belongs to the
Duchess: youвҖҷd better ask her about it.вҖқ вҖңSheвҖҷs in prison,вҖқ the Queen said to the executioner: вҖңfetch her here.вҖқ And the executioner went off like an arrow. The CatвҖҷs head began fading away the moment he was gone, and, by the
time he had come back with the Duchess, it had entirely disappeared; so
the King and the executioner ran wildly up and down looking for it,
while the rest of the party went back to the game.
8-16.prevIllustration: Alice B. Woodward ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Executioner came back with Duchess

9-1.text:
вҖңYou canвҖҷt think how glad I am to see you again, you dear old thing!вҖқ
said the Duchess, as she tucked her arm affectionately into AliceвҖҷs,
and they walked off together.

Alice was very glad to find her in such a pleasant temper, and thought
to herself that perhaps it was only the pepper that had made her so
savage when they met in the kitchen.

вҖңWhen IвҖҷm a Duchess,вҖқ she said to herself, (not in a very hopeful
tone though), вҖңI wonвҖҷt have any pepper in my kitchen at all. Soup
does very well withoutвҖ”Maybe itвҖҷs always pepper that makes people
hot-tempered,вҖқ she went on, very much pleased at having found out a new
kind of rule, вҖңand vinegar that makes them sourвҖ”and camomile that makes
them bitterвҖ”andвҖ”and barley-sugar and such things that make children
sweet-tempered. I only wish people knew that: then they wouldnвҖҷt be
so stingy about it, you knowвҖ”вҖқ

She had quite forgotten the Duchess by this time, and was a little
startled when she heard her voice close to her ear.
9-1.nextIllustration: Gwynedd Hudson ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice & the Duchess

9-2.text:
вҖңYouвҖҷre thinking
about something, my dear, and that makes you forget to talk. I canвҖҷt
tell you just now what the moral of that is, but I shall remember it in
a bit.вҖқ

вҖңPerhaps it hasnвҖҷt one,вҖқ Alice ventured to remark.

вҖңTut, tut, child!вҖқ said the Duchess. вҖңEverythingвҖҷs got a moral, if only
you can find it.вҖқ And she squeezed herself up closer to AliceвҖҷs side as
she spoke.

Alice did not much like keeping so close to her: first, because the
Duchess was very ugly; and secondly, because she was exactly the
right height to rest her chin upon AliceвҖҷs shoulder, and it was an
uncomfortably sharp chin. However, she did not like to be rude, so she
bore it as well as she could.

вҖңThe gameвҖҷs going on rather better now,вҖқ she said, by way of keeping up
the conversation a little.

вҖңвҖҷTis so,вҖқ said the Duchess: вҖңand the moral of that isвҖ”вҖҳOh, вҖҷtis love,
вҖҷtis love, that makes the world go round!вҖҷвҖқ
9-2.illustrationDesc: Gwynedd Hudson ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice & the Duchess

9-3.text:
вҖңSomebody said,вҖқ Alice whispered, вҖңthat itвҖҷs done by everybody minding
their own business!вҖқ

вҖңAh, well! It means much the same thing,вҖқ said the Duchess, digging her
sharp little chin into AliceвҖҷs shoulder as she added, вҖңand the moral of
that isвҖ”вҖҳTake care of the sense, and the sounds will take care of
themselves.вҖҷвҖқ

вҖңHow fond she is of finding morals in things!вҖқ Alice thought to
herself.

вҖңI dare say youвҖҷre wondering why I donвҖҷt put my arm round your waist,вҖқ
the Duchess said after a pause: вҖңthe reason is, that IвҖҷm doubtful about
the temper of your flamingo. Shall I try the experiment?вҖқ

вҖңHe might bite,вҖқ Alice cautiously replied, not feeling at all anxious
to have the experiment tried.

вҖңVery true,вҖқ said the Duchess: вҖңflamingoes and mustard both bite. And
the moral of that isвҖ”вҖҳBirds of a feather flock together.вҖҷвҖқ

вҖңOnly mustard isnвҖҷt a bird,вҖқ Alice remarked.
9-3.prevIllustration: Gwynedd Hudson ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Alice & the Duchess

9-4.text:
вҖңRight, as usual,вҖқ said the Duchess: вҖңwhat a clear way you have of
putting things!вҖқ

вҖңItвҖҷs a mineral, I think,вҖқ said Alice.

вҖңOf course it is,вҖқ said the Duchess, who seemed ready to agree to
everything that Alice said; вҖңthereвҖҷs a large mustard-mine near here.
And the moral of that isвҖ”вҖҳThe more there is of mine, the less there is
of yours.вҖҷвҖқ

вҖңOh, I know!вҖқ exclaimed Alice, who had not attended to this last
remark, вҖңitвҖҷs a vegetable. It doesnвҖҷt look like one, but it is.вҖқ

вҖңI quite agree with you,вҖқ said the Duchess; вҖңand the moral of that
isвҖ”вҖҳBe what you would seem to beвҖҷвҖ”or if youвҖҷd like it put more
simplyвҖ”вҖҳNever imagine yourself not to be otherwise than what it might
appear to others that what you were or might have been was not
otherwise than what you had been would have appeared to them to be
otherwise.вҖҷвҖқ
9-4.nextIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Duchess

9-5.text:
вҖңI think I should understand that better,вҖқ Alice said very politely,
вҖңif I had it written down: but I canвҖҷt quite follow it as you say it.вҖқ

вҖңThatвҖҷs nothing to what I could say if I chose,вҖқ the Duchess replied,
in a pleased tone.

вҖңPray donвҖҷt trouble yourself to say it any longer than that,вҖқ said
Alice.

вҖңOh, donвҖҷt talk about trouble!вҖқ said the Duchess. вҖңI make you a present
of everything IвҖҷve said as yet.вҖқ

вҖңA cheap sort of present!вҖқ thought Alice. вҖңIвҖҷm glad they donвҖҷt give
birthday presents like that!вҖқ But she did not venture to say it out
loud.

вҖңThinking again?вҖқ the Duchess asked, with another dig of her sharp
little chin.

вҖңIвҖҷve a right to think,вҖқ said Alice sharply, for she was beginning to
feel a little worried.
9-5.illustrationDesc: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Duchess
9-5.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll (photograph) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Edith, Ina and Alice, July 1860

9-6.text:
вҖңJust about as much right,вҖқ said the Duchess, вҖңas pigs have to fly; and
the mвҖ”вҖқ

But here, to AliceвҖҷs great surprise, the DuchessвҖҷs voice died away,
even in the middle of her favourite word вҖҳmoral,вҖҷ and the arm that was
linked into hers began to tremble. Alice looked up, and there stood the
Queen in front of them, with her arms folded, frowning like a
thunderstorm.

вҖңA fine day, your Majesty!вҖқ the Duchess began in a low, weak voice.

вҖңNow, I give you fair warning,вҖқ shouted the Queen, stamping on the
ground as she spoke; вҖңeither you or your head must be off, and that in
about half no time! Take your choice!вҖқ

The Duchess took her choice, and was gone in a moment.

вҖңLetвҖҷs go on with the game,вҖқ the Queen said to Alice; and Alice was too
much frightened to say a word, but slowly followed her back to the
croquet-ground.
9-6.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll (photograph) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Edith, Ina and Alice, July 1860
9-6.prevIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Duchess
9-6.nextIllustration: Arthur Rackham ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Gryphon & Mock Turtle

9-7.text:
The other guests had taken advantage of the QueenвҖҷs absence, and were
resting in the shade: however, the moment they saw her, they hurried
back to the game, the Queen merely remarking that a momentвҖҷs delay
would cost them their lives.

All the time they were playing the Queen never left off quarrelling
with the other players, and shouting вҖңOff with his head!вҖқ or вҖңOff with
her head!вҖқ Those whom she sentenced were taken into custody by the
soldiers, who of course had to leave off being arches to do this, so
that by the end of half an hour or so there were no arches left, and
all the players, except the King, the Queen, and Alice, were in custody
and under sentence of execution.

Then the Queen left off, quite out of breath, and said to Alice, вҖңHave
you seen the Mock Turtle yet?вҖқ

вҖңNo,вҖқ said Alice. вҖңI donвҖҷt even know what a Mock Turtle is.вҖқ

вҖңItвҖҷs the thing Mock Turtle Soup is made from,вҖқ said the Queen.
9-7.illustrationDesc: Arthur Rackham ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Gryphon & Mock Turtle
9-7.prevIllustration: Lewis Carroll (photograph) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Edith, Ina and Alice, July 1860

9-8.text:
вҖңI never saw one, or heard of one,вҖқ said Alice.

вҖңCome on, then,вҖқ said the Queen, вҖңand he shall tell you his history.вҖқ

As they walked off together, Alice heard the King say in a low voice,
to the company generally, вҖңYou are all pardoned.вҖқ вҖңCome, thatвҖҷs a
good thing!вҖқ she said to herself, for she had felt quite unhappy at the
number of executions the Queen had ordered.

They very soon came upon a Gryphon, lying fast asleep in the sun. (If
you donвҖҷt know what a Gryphon is, look at the picture.) вҖңUp, lazy
thing!вҖқ said the Queen, вҖңand take this young lady to see the Mock
Turtle, and to hear his history. I must go back and see after some
executions I have ordered;вҖқ and she walked off, leaving Alice alone
with the Gryphon. Alice did not quite like the look of the creature,
but on the whole she thought it would be quite as safe to stay with it
as to go after that savage Queen: so she waited.
9-8.prevIllustration: Arthur Rackham ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Gryphon & Mock Turtle
9-8.nextIllustration: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The griffin sleeps deeply

9-9.text:
The Gryphon sat up and rubbed its eyes: then it watched the Queen till
she was out of sight: then it chuckled. вҖңWhat fun!вҖқ said the Gryphon,
half to itself, half to Alice.

вҖңWhat is the fun?вҖқ said Alice.

вҖңWhy, she,вҖқ said the Gryphon. вҖңItвҖҷs all her fancy, that: they never
executes nobody, you know. Come on!вҖқ

вҖңEverybody says вҖҳcome on!вҖҷ here,вҖқ thought Alice, as she went slowly
after it: вҖңI never was so ordered about in all my life, never!вҖқ

They had not gone far before they saw the Mock Turtle in the distance,
sitting sad and lonely on a little ledge of rock, and, as they came
nearer, Alice could hear him sighing as if his heart would break. She
pitied him deeply. вҖңWhat is his sorrow?вҖқ she asked the Gryphon, and the
Gryphon answered, very nearly in the same words as before, вҖңItвҖҷs all
his fancy, that: he hasnвҖҷt got no sorrow, you know. Come on!вҖқ
9-9.illustrationDesc: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The griffin sleeps deeply

9-10.text:
So they went up to the Mock Turtle, who looked at them with large eyes
full of tears, but said nothing.

вҖңThis here young lady,вҖқ said the Gryphon, вҖңshe wants for to know your
history, she do.вҖқ

вҖңIвҖҷll tell it her,вҖқ said the Mock Turtle in a deep, hollow tone: вҖңsit
down, both of you, and donвҖҷt speak a word till IвҖҷve finished.вҖқ

So they sat down, and nobody spoke for some minutes. Alice thought to
herself, вҖңI donвҖҷt see how he can ever finish, if he doesnвҖҷt begin.вҖқ But she waited patiently.

вҖңOnce,вҖқ said the Mock Turtle at last, with a deep sigh, вҖңI was a real
Turtle.вҖқ

These words were followed by a very long silence, broken only by an
occasional exclamation of вҖңHjckrrh!вҖқ from the Gryphon, and the constant
heavy sobbing of the Mock Turtle. Alice was very nearly getting up and
saying, вҖңThank you, sir, for your interesting story,вҖқ but she could not
help thinking there must be more to come, so she sat still and said
nothing.
9-10.prevIllustration: Brinsley Le Fanu ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The griffin sleeps deeply

9-11.text:
вҖңWhen we were little,вҖқ the Mock Turtle went on at last, more calmly,
though still sobbing a little now and then, вҖңwe went to school in the
sea. The master was an old TurtleвҖ”we used to call him TortoiseвҖ”вҖқ

вҖңWhy did you call him Tortoise, if he wasnвҖҷt one?вҖқ Alice asked.

вҖңWe called him Tortoise because he taught us,вҖқ said the Mock Turtle
angrily: вҖңreally you are very dull!вҖқ

вҖңYou ought to be ashamed of yourself for asking such a simple
question,вҖқ added the Gryphon; and then they both sat silent and looked
at poor Alice, who felt ready to sink into the earth. At last the
Gryphon said to the Mock Turtle, вҖңDrive on, old fellow! DonвҖҷt be all
day about it!вҖқ and he went on in these words:

вҖңYes, we went to school in the sea, though you maynвҖҷt believe itвҖ”вҖқ

вҖңI never said I didnвҖҷt!вҖқ interrupted Alice.

вҖңYou did,вҖқ said the Mock Turtle.

вҖңHold your tongue!вҖқ added the Gryphon, before Alice could speak again. The Mock Turtle went on.

9-12.text:
вҖңWe had the best of educationsвҖ”in fact, we went to school every dayвҖ”вҖқ

вҖңIвҖҷve been to a day-school, too,вҖқ said Alice; вҖңyou neednвҖҷt be so
proud as all that.вҖқ вҖңWith extras?вҖқ asked the Mock Turtle a little anxiously. вҖңYes,вҖқ said Alice, вҖңwe learned French and music.вҖқ вҖңAnd washing?вҖқ said the Mock Turtle. вҖңCertainly not!вҖқ said Alice indignantly. вҖңAh! then yours wasnвҖҷt a really good school,вҖқ said the Mock Turtle in a
tone of great relief. вҖңNow at ours they had at the end of the bill,
вҖҳFrench, music, and washingвҖ”extra.вҖҷвҖқ вҖңYou couldnвҖҷt have wanted it much,вҖқ said Alice; вҖңliving at the bottom
of the sea.вҖқ вҖңI couldnвҖҷt afford to learn it.вҖқ said the Mock Turtle with a sigh. вҖңI
only took the regular course.вҖқ вҖңWhat was that?вҖқ inquired Alice. вҖңReeling and Writhing, of course, to begin with,вҖқ the Mock Turtle
replied; вҖңand then the different branches of ArithmeticвҖ”Ambition,
Distraction, Uglification, and Derision.вҖқ вҖңI never heard of вҖҳUglification,вҖҷвҖқ Alice ventured to say. вҖңWhat is it?вҖқ
9-12.nextIllustration: William H. Walker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Turtle & Gryphon

9-13.text:
The Gryphon lifted up both its paws in surprise. вҖңWhat! Never heard of
uglifying!вҖқ it exclaimed. вҖңYou know what to beautify is, I suppose?вҖқ вҖңYes,вҖқ said Alice doubtfully: вҖңit meansвҖ”toвҖ”makeвҖ”anythingвҖ”prettier.вҖқ вҖңWell, then,вҖқ the Gryphon went on, вҖңif you donвҖҷt know what to uglify
is, you are a simpleton.вҖқ Alice did not feel encouraged to ask any more questions about it, so
she turned to the Mock Turtle, and said вҖңWhat else had you to learn?вҖқ вҖңWell, there was Mystery,вҖқ the Mock Turtle replied, counting off the
subjects on his flappers, вҖңвҖ”Mystery, ancient and modern, with
Seaography: then DrawlingвҖ”the Drawling-master was an old conger-eel,
that used to come once a week: he taught us Drawling, Stretching, and
Fainting in Coils.вҖқ вҖңWhat was that like?вҖқ said Alice. вҖңWell, I canвҖҷt show it you myself,вҖқ the Mock Turtle said: вҖңIвҖҷm too
stiff. And the Gryphon never learnt it.вҖқ вҖңHadnвҖҷt time,вҖқ said the Gryphon: вҖңI went to the Classics master,
though. He was an old crab, he was.вҖқ
9-13.illustrationDesc: William H. Walker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Turtle & Gryphon

9-14.text:
вҖңI never went to him,вҖқ the Mock Turtle said with a sigh: вҖңhe taught
Laughing and Grief, they used to say.вҖқ вҖңSo he did, so he did,вҖқ said the Gryphon, sighing in his turn; and both
creatures hid their faces in their paws. вҖңAnd how many hours a day did you do lessons?вҖқ said Alice, in a hurry
to change the subject. вҖңTen hours the first day,вҖқ said the Mock Turtle: вҖңnine the next, and so
on.вҖқ вҖңWhat a curious plan!вҖқ exclaimed Alice. вҖңThatвҖҷs the reason theyвҖҷre called lessons,вҖқ the Gryphon remarked:
вҖңbecause they lessen from day to day.вҖқ This was quite a new idea to Alice, and she thought it over a little
before she made her next remark. вҖңThen the eleventh day must have been
a holiday?вҖқ вҖңOf course it was,вҖқ said the Mock Turtle. вҖңAnd how did you manage on the twelfth?вҖқ Alice went on eagerly. вҖңThatвҖҷs enough about lessons,вҖқ the Gryphon interrupted in a very
decided tone: вҖңtell her something about the games now.вҖқ
9-14.prevIllustration: William H. Walker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Turtle & Gryphon

10-1.text:
The Mock Turtle sighed deeply, and drew the back of one flapper across
his eyes. He looked at Alice, and tried to speak, but for a minute or
two sobs choked his voice. вҖңSame as if he had a bone in his throat,вҖқ
said the Gryphon: and it set to work shaking him and punching him in
the back. At last the Mock Turtle recovered his voice, and, with tears
running down his cheeks, he went on again:вҖ”

вҖңYou may not have lived much under the seaвҖ”вҖқ (вҖңI havenвҖҷt,вҖқ said
Alice)вҖ”вҖңand perhaps you were never even introduced to a lobsterвҖ”вҖқ
(Alice began to say вҖңI once tastedвҖ”вҖқ but checked herself hastily, and
said вҖңNo, neverвҖқ) вҖңвҖ”so you can have no idea what a delightful thing a
Lobster Quadrille is!вҖқ

вҖңNo, indeed,вҖқ said Alice. вҖңWhat sort of a dance is it?вҖқ

вҖңWhy,вҖқ said the Gryphon, вҖңyou first form into a line along the
sea-shoreвҖ”вҖқ

вҖңTwo lines!вҖқ cried the Mock Turtle. вҖңSeals, turtles, salmon, and so on;
then, when youвҖҷve cleared all the jelly-fish out of the wayвҖ”вҖқ

вҖңThat generally takes some time,вҖқ interrupted the Gryphon.

10-2.text:
вҖңвҖ”you advance twiceвҖ”вҖқ

вҖңEach with a lobster as a partner!вҖқ cried the Gryphon.

вҖңOf course,вҖқ the Mock Turtle said: вҖңadvance twice, set to partnersвҖ”вҖқ

вҖңвҖ”change lobsters, and retire in same order,вҖқ continued the Gryphon.

вҖңThen, you know,вҖқ the Mock Turtle went on, вҖңyou throw theвҖ”вҖқ

вҖңThe lobsters!вҖқ shouted the Gryphon, with a bound into the air.

вҖңвҖ”as far out to sea as you canвҖ”вҖқ

вҖңSwim after them!вҖқ screamed the Gryphon.

вҖңTurn a somersault in the sea!вҖқ cried the Mock Turtle, capering wildly
about.

вҖңChange lobsters again!вҖқ yelled the Gryphon at the top of its voice.

вҖңBack to land again, and thatвҖҷs all the first figure,вҖқ said the Mock
Turtle, suddenly dropping his voice; and the two creatures, who had
been jumping about like mad things all this time, sat down again very
sadly and quietly, and looked at Alice.

вҖңIt must be a very pretty dance,вҖқ said Alice timidly.

10-3.text:
вҖңWould you like to see a little of it?вҖқ said the Mock Turtle.

вҖңVery much indeed,вҖқ said Alice.

вҖңCome, letвҖҷs try the first figure!вҖқ said the Mock Turtle to the
Gryphon. вҖңWe can do without lobsters, you know. Which shall sing?вҖқ

вҖңOh, you sing,вҖқ said the Gryphon. вҖңIвҖҷve forgotten the words.вҖқ

So they began solemnly dancing round and round Alice, every now and
then treading on her toes when they passed too close, and waving their
forepaws to mark the time, while the Mock Turtle sang this, very slowly
and sadly:вҖ”

вҖңWill you walk a little faster?вҖқ said a whiting to a snail. вҖңThereвҖҷs a porpoise close behind us, and heвҖҷs treading on my tail.
See how eagerly the lobsters and the turtles all advance!
They are waiting on the shingleвҖ”will you come and join the dance?
Will you, wonвҖҷt you, will you, wonвҖҷt you, will you join the dance?
Will you, wonвҖҷt you, will you, wonвҖҷt you, wonвҖҷt you join the dance?

10-4.text:
вҖңYou can really have no notion how delightful it will be
When they take us up and throw us, with the lobsters, out to sea!вҖқ But the snail replied вҖңToo far, too far!вҖқ and gave a look askanceвҖ”
Said he thanked the whiting kindly, but he would not join the dance. Would not, could not, would not, could not, would not join the dance. Would not, could not, would not, could not, could not join the dance.

вҖңWhat matters it how far we go?вҖқ his scaly friend replied. вҖңThere is another shore, you know, upon the other side.
The further off from England the nearer is to FranceвҖ”
Then turn not pale, beloved snail, but come and join the dance.
Will you, wonвҖҷt you, will you, wonвҖҷt you, will you join the dance?
Will you, wonвҖҷt you, will you, wonвҖҷt you, wonвҖҷt you join the dance?вҖқ

10-5.text:
вҖңThank you, itвҖҷs a very interesting dance to watch,вҖқ said Alice,
feeling very glad that it was over at last: вҖңand I do so like that
curious song about the whiting!вҖқ

вҖңOh, as to the whiting,вҖқ said the Mock Turtle, вҖңtheyвҖ”youвҖҷve seen them,
of course?вҖқ

вҖңYes,вҖқ said Alice, вҖңIвҖҷve often seen them at dinnвҖ”вҖқ she checked herself
hastily.

вҖңI donвҖҷt know where Dinn may be,вҖқ said the Mock Turtle, вҖңbut if youвҖҷve
seen them so often, of course you know what theyвҖҷre like.вҖқ

вҖңI believe so,вҖқ Alice replied thoughtfully. вҖңThey have their tails in
their mouthsвҖ”and theyвҖҷre all over crumbs.вҖқ

вҖңYouвҖҷre wrong about the crumbs,вҖқ said the Mock Turtle: вҖңcrumbs would
all wash off in the sea. But they have their tails in their mouths;
and the reason isвҖ”вҖқ here the Mock Turtle yawned and shut his
eyes.вҖ”вҖңTell her about the reason and all that,вҖқ he said to the Gryphon.

10-6.text:
вҖңThe reason is,вҖқ said the Gryphon, вҖңthat they would go with the
lobsters to the dance. So they got thrown out to sea. So they had to
fall a long way. So they got their tails fast in their mouths. So they
couldnвҖҷt get them out again. ThatвҖҷs all.вҖқ

вҖңThank you,вҖқ said Alice, вҖңitвҖҷs very interesting. I never knew so much
about a whiting before.вҖқ

вҖңI can tell you more than that, if you like,вҖқ said the Gryphon. вҖңDo you
know why itвҖҷs called a whiting?вҖқ

вҖңI never thought about it,вҖқ said Alice. вҖңWhy?вҖқ

вҖңIt does the boots and shoes,вҖқ the Gryphon replied very solemnly.

Alice was thoroughly puzzled. вҖңDoes the boots and shoes!вҖқ she repeated
in a wondering tone.

вҖңWhy, what are your shoes done with?вҖқ said the Gryphon. вҖңI mean, what
makes them so shiny?вҖқ

Alice looked down at them, and considered a little before she gave her
answer. вҖңTheyвҖҷre done with blacking, I believe.вҖқ
10-6.nextIllustration: Photograph ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” A grown up Alice of Wonderland

10-7.text:
вҖңBoots and shoes under the sea,вҖқ the Gryphon went on in a deep voice,
вҖңare done with a whiting. Now you know.вҖқ

вҖңAnd what are they made of?вҖқ Alice asked in a tone of great curiosity.

вҖңSoles and eels, of course,вҖқ the Gryphon replied rather impatiently:
вҖңany shrimp could have told you that.вҖқ

вҖңIf IвҖҷd been the whiting,вҖқ said Alice, whose thoughts were still
running on the song, вҖңIвҖҷd have said to the porpoise, вҖҳKeep back,
please: we donвҖҷt want you with us!вҖҷвҖқ

вҖңThey were obliged to have him with them,вҖқ the Mock Turtle said: вҖңno
wise fish would go anywhere without a porpoise.вҖқ

вҖңWouldnвҖҷt it really?вҖқ said Alice in a tone of great surprise.

вҖңOf course not,вҖқ said the Mock Turtle: вҖңwhy, if a fish came to me,
and told me he was going a journey, I should say вҖҳWith what porpoise?вҖҷвҖқ

вҖңDonвҖҷt you mean вҖҳpurposeвҖҷ?вҖқ said Alice.

вҖңI mean what I say,вҖқ the Mock Turtle replied in an offended tone. And
the Gryphon added вҖңCome, letвҖҷs hear some of your adventures.вҖқ
10-7.illustrationDesc: Photograph ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” A grown up Alice of Wonderland

10-8.text:
вҖңI could tell you my adventuresвҖ”beginning from this morning,вҖқ said
Alice a little timidly: вҖңbut itвҖҷs no use going back to yesterday,
because I was a different person then.вҖқ

вҖңExplain all that,вҖқ said the Mock Turtle.

вҖңNo, no! The adventures first,вҖқ said the Gryphon in an impatient tone:
вҖңexplanations take such a dreadful time.вҖқ

So Alice began telling them her adventures from the time when she first
saw the White Rabbit. She was a little nervous about it just at first,
the two creatures got so close to her, one on each side, and opened
their eyes and mouths so very wide, but she gained courage as she
went on. Her listeners were perfectly quiet till she got to the part
about her repeating вҖңYou are old, Father William,вҖқ to the
Caterpillar, and the words all coming different, and then the Mock
Turtle drew a long breath, and said вҖңThatвҖҷs very curious.вҖқ
10-8.prevIllustration: Photograph ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” A grown up Alice of Wonderland
10-8.nextIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The White Rabbit

10-9.text:
вҖңItвҖҷs all about as curious as it can be,вҖқ said the Gryphon.

вҖңIt all came different!вҖқ the Mock Turtle repeated thoughtfully. вҖңI
should like to hear her try and repeat something now. Tell her to
begin.вҖқ He looked at the Gryphon as if he thought it had some kind of
authority over Alice.

вҖңStand up and repeat вҖҳвҖҷTis the voice of the sluggard,вҖҷвҖқ said the
Gryphon.

вҖңHow the creatures order one about, and make one repeat lessons!вҖқ
thought Alice; вҖңI might as well be at school at once.вҖқ However, she got
up, and began to repeat it, but her head was so full of the Lobster
Quadrille, that she hardly knew what she was saying, and the words came
very queer indeed:вҖ”

вҖңвҖҷTis the voice of the Lobster; I heard him declare,
вҖңYou have baked me too brown, I must sugar my hair.вҖқ
As a duck with its eyelids, so he with his nose
Trims his belt and his buttons, and turns out his toes.вҖқ
10-9.illustrationDesc: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The White Rabbit

10-10.text:
[later editions continued as follows
When the sands are all dry, he is gay as a lark,
And will talk in contemptuous tones of the Shark,
But, when the tide rises and sharks are around,
His voice has a timid and tremulous sound.]

вҖңThatвҖҷs different from what I used to say when I was a child,вҖқ said
the Gryphon.

вҖңWell, I never heard it before,вҖқ said the Mock Turtle; вҖңbut it sounds
uncommon nonsense.вҖқ

Alice said nothing; she had sat down with her face in her hands,
wondering if anything would ever happen in a natural way again.

вҖңI should like to have it explained,вҖқ said the Mock Turtle.

вҖңShe canвҖҷt explain it,вҖқ said the Gryphon hastily. вҖңGo on with the next
verse.вҖқ

вҖңBut about his toes?вҖқ the Mock Turtle persisted. вҖңHow could he turn
them out with his nose, you know?вҖқ

вҖңItвҖҷs the first position in dancing.вҖқ Alice said; but was dreadfully
puzzled by the whole thing, and longed to change the subject.
10-10.prevIllustration: John Tenniel (colorized E.G. Thomson) ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The White Rabbit
10-10.nextIllustration: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Lobster dancing

10-11.text:
вҖңGo on with the next verse,вҖқ the Gryphon repeated impatiently: вҖңit
begins вҖҳI passed by his garden.вҖҷвҖқ Alice did not dare to disobey, though she felt sure it would all come
wrong, and she went on in a trembling voice:вҖ”

вҖңI passed by his garden, and marked, with one eye,
How the Owl and the Panther were sharing a pieвҖ”вҖқ

[later editions continued as follows
The Panther took pie-crust, and gravy, and meat,
While the Owl had the dish as its share of the treat. When the pie was all finished, the Owl, as a boon,
Was kindly permitted to pocket the spoon:
While the Panther received knife and fork with a growl,
And concluded the banquetвҖ”]

вҖңWhat is the use of repeating all that stuff,вҖқ the Mock Turtle
interrupted, вҖңif you donвҖҷt explain it as you go on? ItвҖҷs by far the
most confusing thing I ever heard!вҖқ вҖңYes, I think youвҖҷd better leave off,вҖқ said the Gryphon: and Alice was
only too glad to do so. вҖңShall we try another figure of the Lobster Quadrille?вҖқ the Gryphon
went on.
10-11.illustrationDesc: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Lobster dancing

10-12.text:
вҖңOr would you like the Mock Turtle to sing you a song?вҖқ вҖңOh, a song, please, if the Mock Turtle would be so kind,вҖқ Alice
replied, so eagerly that the Gryphon said, in a rather offended tone,
вҖңHm! No accounting for tastes! Sing her вҖҳTurtle Soup,вҖҷ will you, old
fellow?вҖқ The Mock Turtle sighed deeply, and began, in a voice sometimes choked
with sobs, to sing this:вҖ”

вҖңBeautiful Soup, so rich and green,
Waiting in a hot tureen!
Who for such dainties would not stoop?
Soup of the evening, beautiful Soup!
Soup of the evening, beautiful Soup!
    BeauвҖ”ootiful SooвҖ”oop!
    BeauвҖ”ootiful SooвҖ”oop!
SooвҖ”oop of the eвҖ”eвҖ”evening,
    Beautiful, beautiful Soup! вҖңBeautiful Soup! Who cares for fish,
Game, or any other dish?
Who would not give all else for two p
ennyworth only of beautiful Soup?
Pennyworth only of beautiful Soup?
    BeauвҖ”ootiful SooвҖ”oop!
    BeauвҖ”ootiful SooвҖ”oop!
SooвҖ”oop of the eвҖ”eвҖ”evening,
    Beautiful, beautiвҖ”FUL SOUP!вҖқ
10-12.prevIllustration: Lewis Carroll ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” The Lobster dancing

10-13.text:
вҖңChorus again!вҖқ cried the Gryphon, and the Mock Turtle had just begun
to repeat it, when a cry of вҖңThe trialвҖҷs beginning!вҖқ was heard in the
distance. вҖңCome on!вҖқ cried the Gryphon, and, taking Alice by the hand, it hurried
off, without waiting for the end of the song. вҖңWhat trial is it?вҖқ Alice panted as she ran; but the Gryphon only
answered вҖңCome on!вҖқ and ran the faster, while more and more faintly
came, carried on the breeze that followed them, the melancholy words:вҖ”

вҖңSooвҖ”oop of the eвҖ”eвҖ”evening,
    Beautiful, beautiful Soup!вҖқ

11-1.text:
The King and Queen of Hearts were seated on their throne when they
arrived, with a great crowd assembled about themвҖ”all sorts of little
birds and beasts, as well as the whole pack of cards: the Knave was
standing before them, in chains, with a soldier on each side to guard
him; and near the King was the White Rabbit, with a trumpet in one
hand, and a scroll of parchment in the other. In the very middle of the
court was a table, with a large dish of tarts upon it: they looked so
good, that it made Alice quite hungry to look at themвҖ”вҖңI wish theyвҖҷd
get the trial done,вҖқ she thought, вҖңand hand round the refreshments!вҖқ But there seemed to be no chance of this, so she began looking at
everything about her, to pass away the time.

Alice had never been in a court of justice before, but she had read
about them in books, and she was quite pleased to find that she knew
the name of nearly everything there. вҖңThatвҖҷs the judge,вҖқ she said to
herself, вҖңbecause of his great wig.вҖқ
11-1.nextIllustration: Arthur Rackham ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Knave of Spades

11-2.text:
The judge, by the way, was the King; and as he wore his crown over the
wig, (look at the frontispiece if you want to see how he did it,) he
did not look at all comfortable, and it was certainly not becoming.

вҖңAnd thatвҖҷs the jury-box,вҖқ thought Alice, вҖңand those twelve creatures,вҖқ
(she was obliged to say вҖңcreatures,вҖқ you see, because some of them were
animals, and some were birds,) вҖңI suppose they are the jurors.вҖқ She
said this last word two or three times over to herself, being rather
proud of it: for she thought, and rightly too, that very few little
girls of her age knew the meaning of it at all. However, вҖңjury-menвҖқ
would have done just as well.

The twelve jurors were all writing very busily on slates. вҖңWhat are
they doing?вҖқ Alice whispered to the Gryphon. вҖңThey canвҖҷt have anything
to put down yet, before the trialвҖҷs begun.вҖқ
11-2.illustrationDesc: Arthur Rackham ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Knave of Spades

11-3.text:
вҖңTheyвҖҷre putting down their names,вҖқ the Gryphon whispered in reply,
вҖңfor fear they should forget them before the end of the trial.вҖқ

вҖңStupid things!вҖқ Alice began in a loud, indignant voice, but she
stopped hastily, for the White Rabbit cried out, вҖңSilence in the
court!вҖқ and the King put on his spectacles and looked anxiously round,
to make out who was talking.

Alice could see, as well as if she were looking over their shoulders,
that all the jurors were writing down вҖңstupid things!вҖқ on their slates,
and she could even make out that one of them didnвҖҷt know how to spell
вҖңstupid,вҖқ and that he had to ask his neighbour to tell him. вҖңA nice
muddle their slatesвҖҷll be in before the trialвҖҷs over!вҖқ thought Alice.

One of the jurors had a pencil that squeaked. This of course, Alice
could not stand, and she went round the court and got behind him, and
very soon found an opportunity of taking it away.
11-3.prevIllustration: Arthur Rackham ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” Knave of Spades
11-3.nextIllustration: William H. Walker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” In court

11-4.text:
She did it so quickly
that the poor little juror (it was Bill, the Lizard) could not make out
at all what had become of it; so, after hunting all about for it, he
was obliged to write with one finger for the rest of the day; and this
was of very little use, as it left no mark on the slate.

вҖңHerald, read the accusation!вҖқ said the King.

On this the White Rabbit blew three blasts on the trumpet, and then
unrolled the parchment scroll, and read as follows:вҖ”

вҖңThe Queen of Hearts, she made some tarts,
    All on a summer day:
The Knave of Hearts, he stole those tarts,
    And took them quite away!вҖқ

вҖңConsider your verdict,вҖқ the King said to the jury.

вҖңNot yet, not yet!вҖқ the Rabbit hastily interrupted. вҖңThereвҖҷs a great
deal to come before that!вҖқ

вҖңCall the first witness,вҖқ said the King; and the White Rabbit blew
three blasts on the trumpet, and called out, вҖңFirst witness!вҖқ
11-4.illustrationDesc: William H. Walker ГғВўГӮВҖГӮВ” In court

11-5.text:
The first witness was the Hatter. He came in with a teacup in one hand
and a piece of bread-and-butter in the other. вҖңI beg pardon, your
Majesty,вҖқ he began, вҖңfor bringing these in: but I hadnвҖҷt quite finished
my tea when I was sent for.вҖқ

вҖңYou ought to have finished,вҖқ said the King. вҖңWhen did you begin?вҖқ

The Hatter looked at the March Hare, who had followed him into the
court, arm-in-arm with the Dormouse. вҖңFourteenth of March, I think it
was,вҖқ he said.

вҖңFifteenth,вҖқ said the March Hare.

вҖңSixteenth,вҖқ added the Dormouse.

вҖңWrite that down,вҖқ the King said to the jury, and the jury eagerly
